CTG demonstrates a reactive trace without decelerations. The junior resident asks whether antihypertensive adjustment alone is sufficient because the foetal status currently appears reassuring.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 174/112 mmHg, HR 96 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 36.8 C
data_table.Examination: Brisk reflexes with 2-beat clonus, right upper quadrant tenderness, mild pedal oedema
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 108 x10^9/L, ALT 96 U/L, creatinine 102 micromol/L, urine protein:creatinine ratio elevated

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Increase oral antihypertensive therapy and continue expectant management because CTG remains reassuring
options.rationale: This patient has severe pre-eclampsia features including neurological symptoms, severe hypertension, thrombocytopenia, and liver involvement requiring escalation beyond oral antihypertensives alone. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension Guideline)

options.text: Commence magnesium sulfate and plan expedited delivery after maternal stabilisation
options.rationale: Severe pre-eclampsia with neurological symptoms and end-organ involvement requires seizure prophylaxis and delivery planning after stabilisation. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension Guideline)

options.text: Administer corticosteroids and delay delivery for at least 7 days because prematurity risk outweighs maternal concerns
options.rationale: Maternal severe disease at 35 weeks generally outweighs benefits of prolonged expectant management. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertensive Disorders)

options.text: Arrange outpatient blood pressure review tomorrow because foetal wellbeing remains reassuring
options.rationale: This degree of hypertension and end-organ involvement requires inpatient urgent management rather than outpatient follow-up. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension Guideline)

options.text: Commence intravenous antibiotics because thrombocytopenia and transaminitis suggest occult sepsis
options.rationale: The overall pattern strongly supports severe pre-eclampsia rather than primary sepsis. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertensive Disorders)

explanation.summary: This patient has severe pre-eclampsia with neurological symptoms, severe hypertension, thrombocytopenia, liver involvement, and renal impairment. Maternal stabilisation with magnesium sulfate and planning for delivery are required despite reassuring foetal monitoring. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension Guideline)
key_points: Reassuring CTG does not negate severe maternal disease. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertensive Disorders)
Magnesium sulfate reduces eclampsia risk in severe pre-eclampsia. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Right upper quadrant pain in pre-eclampsia may reflect hepatic involvement and impending severe complications. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension Guideline)

stem: A 31-year-old multiparous woman undergoes spontaneous vaginal delivery of a healthy infant at 39 weeks gestation after induction for gestational diabetes. The placenta delivers intact 10 minutes later. Shortly afterwards she develops ongoing heavy vaginal bleeding with large clots. Uterine massage is attempted while additional staff are called. She received oxytocin during labour augmentation. The registrar notes the uterus feels enlarged and boggy above the umbilicus. There is no obvious genital tract trauma on initial inspection. A junior doctor suggests waiting briefly because the patient remains awake and conversational.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 92/56 mmHg, HR 132 bpm, RR 24/min, Temp 36.7 C, SpO2 98% room air
data_table.Examination: Boggy enlarged uterus, ongoing brisk vaginal bleeding, pallor
data_table.Investigations: Estimated blood loss 1400 mL so far

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next intervention?

options.text: Perform immediate hysterectomy because severe postpartum bleeding rarely responds to medical management
options.rationale: Hysterectomy may become necessary later but initial management of uterine atony involves uterotonics and resuscitative measures. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Commence uterotonic therapy while continuing uterine massage and haemodynamic resuscitation
options.rationale: The boggy enlarged uterus strongly suggests uterine atony, which requires prompt uterotonics, massage, and resuscitation. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Observe for spontaneous uterine contraction because the patient remains conscious and responsive
options.rationale: Ongoing heavy bleeding with haemodynamic compromise requires urgent active management rather than observation. (Source: RWH Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

options.text: Arrange urgent CT angiography because retained products are the most likely cause of bleeding
options.rationale: The boggy uterus and immediate postpartum timing strongly support uterine atony rather than a primary need for imaging. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Prioritise broad-spectrum antibiotics because postpartum endometritis commonly causes major haemorrhage
options.rationale: This acute heavy postpartum bleeding pattern with uterine atony is not typical of endometritis. (Source: RWH Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

explanation.summary: This patient has major postpartum haemorrhage due to uterine atony, evidenced by a boggy enlarged uterus and heavy bleeding after placental delivery. Immediate uterotonics, uterine massage, and haemodynamic resuscitation are essential first-line interventions. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage)
key_points: Uterine atony is the most common cause of postpartum haemorrhage. (Source: RWH Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
Management requires simultaneous haemorrhage control and resuscitation. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage)
explanation.pearls: A patient may initially appear conversational despite life-threatening postpartum haemorrhage because young healthy patients compensate physiologically early. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage)

stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 at 38+2 weeks gestation presents in spontaneous labour. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated. On admission 30 minutes ago, cervical examination was 5 cm dilated with intact membranes, cephalic presentation at station -1. She is now reclining in bed and reports a sudden gush of clear fluid followed by a sensation of something in her vagina. On examination, you palpate a pulsatile, soft, looped structure presenting at the introitus. The foetal heart rate, which was previously 140 bpm with good variability, is now 70 bpm and has remained there for 90 seconds despite repositioning the woman into left lateral. Tocolysis with subcutaneous terbutaline 250 mcg has been administered. The obstetric registrar and theatre team have been called and are 5 minutes away. The woman is visibly distressed and repeatedly asks if her baby will be okay.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal HR 102 bpm, BP 125/80 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 98% on room air
data_table.Examination: Visible umbilical cord at introitus, pulsatile, no presenting part visible, cervix fully assessed at 6 cm, station -2
data_table.Investigations: CTG: sustained foetal bradycardia at 70 bpm for 90 seconds, no recovery with position change
data_table.Medications: Terbutaline 250 mcg SC administered 2 minutes ago

lead_in: What is the SINGLE MOST appropriate next step while awaiting theatre?

options.text: Perform a digital vaginal examination to assess cervical dilation and exclude footling breech
options.rationale: Digital examination of a cord prolapse risks further cord compression, spasm, and worsens foetal hypoxia. The priority is continuous elevation of the presenting part without manipulating the cord. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Elevate the presenting part continuously with a hand in the vagina and fill the bladder with 500–700 mL of normal saline via catheter
options.rationale: Continuous manual elevation of the presenting part off the cord combined with rapid tocolysis and bladder filling (retrograde filling of the bladder) is the definitive temporising measure to reduce cord compression while preparing for immediate delivery. The presenting part is lifted cephalad and the distended bladder helps maintain that displacement. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse; KEMH Clinical Guidelines: Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Administer a second dose of terbutaline 250 mcg SC and place the woman in the Trendelenburg position
options.rationale: Tocolysis has already been administered, and a second dose is unlikely to improve the situation and may cause maternal tachycardia without foetal benefit. Trendelenburg position alone is insufficient to relieve cord compression. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Prompt the woman to push actively to deliver the foetus as quickly as possible vaginally
options.rationale: The cervix is 6 cm dilated and the presenting part is at station -2; vaginal delivery is not imminent. Encouraging pushing will worsen cord compression by driving the presenting part downward. The priority is emergency caesarean section after cord decompression measures. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Attempt manual replacement of the cord into the uterine cavity to restore foetal circulation
options.rationale: Manual replacement of a prolapsed cord is no longer recommended as it is rarely successful, traumatises the cord, and delays definitive management. Continuous elevation of the presenting part and rapid delivery is the standard. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)

explanation.summary: This woman has a cord prolapse following spontaneous rupture of membranes at 6 cm dilation with a high presenting part (station -2). The sustained foetal bradycardia indicates cord compression. While awaiting emergency caesarean section, the priority is continuous manual elevation of the presenting part to decompress the cord, combined with bladder filling to maintain that elevation. Tocolysis has already been given to reduce uterine activity.
key_points: Cord prolapse is an obstetric emergency requiring continuous elevation of the presenting part off the cord until delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)
Bladder filling with 500–700 mL normal saline via catheter is an effective adjunct to manual elevation while preparing for theatre. (Source: KEMH Clinical Guidelines: Cord Prolapse)
Digital examination or manipulation of the cord should be avoided as it can provoke vasospasm and worsen foetal hypoxia. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)
Decision-to-delivery interval target is <30 minutes when foetal compromise is present, but cord decompression measures must continue throughout. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)
explanation.pearls: The hand on the presenting part stays in the vagina until the surgeon is ready to make the uterine incision; do not remove it for transfer, catheterisation, or any other intervention.

subtopic: Antepartum haemorrhage – Placenta praevia vs accreta spectrum
stem: A 36-year-old G4P2A1 at 32+1 weeks gestation presents to the birth suite with sudden onset of bright red vaginal bleeding approximately 1 hour ago. She reports no pain and no contractions. The bleeding was initially moderate but has now reduced to spotting. Her obstetric history includes two previous lower-segment caesarean sections (the most recent 2 years ago) and a first-trimester miscarriage managed with suction curettage. At her 20-week morphology scan, the placenta was noted to be posterior and covering the internal os completely. A follow-up scan at 28 weeks confirmed a major placenta praevia. Her antenatal course has been otherwise uncomplicated. On examination, her abdomen is soft and non-tender with a singleton foetus in cephalic presentation. The fundal height is consistent with dates. Speculum examination reveals a closed cervix with a small amount of dark blood in the posterior fornix. Foetal cardiotocography shows a baseline of 145 bpm with normal variability, no decelerations, and no uterine activity. Her haemoglobin is 102 g/L (down from 118 g/L at 28 weeks) and haematocrit is 0.31. The estimated blood loss is approximately 400 mL. She is haemodynamically stable with HR 88 bpm and BP 110/70 mmHg. She has received betamethasone 11.4 mg IM 24 hours apart with the second dose given 12 hours ago.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal HR 88 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 98%, Temp 36.8°C
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, non-tender, fundal height = dates, foetus cephalic, speculum: closed cervix, dark blood posterior fornix
data_table.Investigations: Hb 102 g/L (was 118 at 28 wks), Hct 0.31, CTG baseline 145 bpm normal variability no decelerations no contractions

lead_in: Which risk factor in this presentation most strongly raises concern for placenta accreta spectrum disorder over an uncomplicated major placenta praevia?

options.text: Maternal age over 35 years at the time of delivery
options.rationale: Advanced maternal age modestly increases the risk of placenta praevia and accreta, but the magnitude of risk from age alone is far less than that conferred by the combination of praevia with prior uterine surgery. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: The confirmed persistence of a major placenta praevia at 28 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Persistent major placenta praevia at 28 weeks increases the risk of PAS compared to a resolved praevia, but it is the combination with prior caesarean sections that most strongly predicts accreta. Isolated praevia without prior uterine surgery carries a PAS risk of approximately 3%. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: First-trimester miscarriage managed with suction curettage in addition to the two prior caesarean sections
options.rationale: While suction curettage does cause endometrial disruption and is an additional risk factor, the dominant driver of PAS risk is the combination of placenta praevia with prior caesarean sections. The curettage adds a minor additional risk but is not the strongest predictor. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: The combination of major placenta praevia with two previous lower-segment caesarean sections
options.rationale: The risk of placenta accreta spectrum rises dramatically with the number of prior caesarean sections in the setting of placenta praevia. With no prior CS, the risk is approximately 3%; with one prior CS, 11%; with two prior CS, 40%; with three prior CS, 67%. This exponential rise makes the combination the strongest risk factor. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum; FIGO Consensus Guidelines on PAS)

options.text: The drop in haemoglobin from 118 g/L at 28 weeks to 102 g/L at presentation with bright red bleeding
options.rationale: A drop in haemoglobin reflects blood loss from the current episode of bleeding and is a marker of haemorrhage severity, not a risk factor for PAS. PAS risk is determined by pre-existing uterine factors rather than the clinical presentation of bleeding. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

explanation.summary: This question asks the student to identify the strongest risk factor for placenta accreta spectrum (PAS) among the features presented. The exponential relationship between the number of prior caesarean sections and PAS risk in the setting of placenta praevia is the most important clinical predictor. The two prior CS with a persistent major praevia places this woman at approximately 40% risk of PAS, which mandates specific delivery planning including senior surgical presence, blood product availability, and consideration of a multidisciplinary team approach.
key_points: PAS risk with placenta praevia increases exponentially with number of prior caesarean sections: ~3% with none, ~11% with one, ~40% with two, ~67% with three. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

Antenatal diagnosis of PAS relies on grey-scale and colour Doppler ultrasound with findings of loss of the retroplacental clear zone, placental lacunae, and increased vascularity. (Source: FIGO Consensus Guidelines on PAS)
All women with placenta praevia and prior caesarean delivery should have a targeted ultrasound for PAS at 28–32 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

Suspected PAS should prompt referral to a tertiary centre with multidisciplinary capability including MFM, anaesthetics, interventional radiology, and massive transfusion protocol. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum)
explanation.pearls: When a woman with prior CS and placenta praevia bleeds, do not perform a digital vaginal examination - it can provoke catastrophic haemorrhage if there is an undiagnosed accreta or praevia.

stem: A 29-year-old primigravida at 40+0 weeks gestation is in the second stage of labour. She has been pushing for 90 minutes with an epidural in situ. Her pregnancy was complicated by diet-controlled gestational diabetes diagnosed at 28 weeks on the 75 g OGTT (fasting 5.3 mmol/L, 1-hour 10.2 mmol/L, 2-hour 8.6 mmol/L). Foetal surveillance has been normal. The head has delivered spontaneously over an intact perineum. Following delivery of the head, the chin retracts tightly against the perineum (turtle-neck sign) and restitution does not occur. Gentle downward traction fails to deliver the anterior shoulder. The accoucheur calls for help. The woman has been moved to the end of the bed and her legs are placed in hyperflexion (McRoberts' position). After one further attempt at downward traction, the anterior shoulder remains undelivered. Suprapubic pressure has been applied by an assistant using a closed fist in a downward and lateral direction with the next contraction. The shoulder has still not delivered. The paediatric team is present and the time since delivery of the head is now 2 minutes and 30 seconds.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal HR 110 bpm, BP 135/85 mmHg, foetal HR at last assessment 130 bpm pre-push
data_table.Examination: Turtle-neck sign present, restitution absent, head tightly applied to perineum, McRoberts' position achieved, suprapubic pressure unsuccessful
data_table.Investigations: GDM: 75 g OGTT fasting 5.3, 1h 10.2, 2h 8.6 mmol/L (diagnostic per ADIPS criteria)
data_table.Medications: Epidural in situ (bupivacaine 0.1% + fentanyl 2 mcg/mL at 8 mL/hr)

lead_in: What is the SINGLE BEST next manoeuvre to attempt?

options.text: Apply firmer downward traction on the foetal head combined with continuous suprapubic pressure for a full 60 seconds
options.rationale: Increasing traction force risks brachial plexus injury and does not address the underlying impaction of the anterior shoulder behind the symphysis pubis. After McRoberts' position and suprapubic pressure have failed, internal manoeuvres are required. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Deliver the posterior arm by entering the sacral hollow, locating the humerus, sweeping it across the chest, and delivering the arm
options.rationale: When McRoberts' position and suprapubic pressure fail to resolve shoulder dystocia, delivery of the posterior arm is the next recommended step in the HELPERR mnemonic. This reduces the bisacromial diameter by up to 2 cm and has a high success rate. The procedure involves entering posteriorly along the sacral curve, splinting the humerus, and sweeping the arm across the foetal chest. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia; KEMH: Shoulder Dystocia Guideline)

options.text: Rotate the foetus by applying pressure to the anterior shoulder through the abdominal wall (Rubin I manoeuvre)
options.rationale: Rubin I (external rotation of the anterior shoulder via abdominal pressure) is a variant of suprapubic pressure and has already been functionally attempted. When external manoeuvres have failed, internal rotational manoeuvres (Rubin II or Woods' screw) or posterior arm delivery are indicated. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Perform an episiotomy to increase the available space for delivery before attempting further traction
options.rationale: Shoulder dystocia is a bony impaction problem, not a soft-tissue obstruction. Episiotomy alone will not relieve the impaction of the anterior shoulder behind the symphysis pubis. It may be performed to facilitate access for internal manoeuvres, but it is not a manoeuvre in itself and is not the next priority. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Perform the Gaskin all-fours manoeuvre by rolling the woman onto her hands and knees
options.rationale: The Gaskin manoeuvre (all-fours position) can be effective and is sometimes used earlier in the sequence. However, with an epidural in situ, moving the woman to all-fours is difficult and potentially unsafe. In an Australian tertiary setting with epidural, internal manoeuvres (posterior arm delivery or internal rotation) are the next step after failed McRoberts and suprapubic pressure. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

explanation.summary: This primigravida with GDM (a known risk factor for shoulder dystocia) has a recognised shoulder dystocia with the classic turtle-neck sign and failure of restitution. McRoberts' position and suprapubic pressure (the first two HELPERR steps) have been correctly applied without success. The next step is delivery of the posterior arm, which is highly effective and reduces the bisacromial diameter. Time from head delivery is critical and internal manoeuvres should not be delayed beyond 3-4 minutes.
key_points: The HELPERR mnemonic guides sequential management: Help, Evaluate for episiotomy, Legs (McRoberts'), Pressure (suprapubic), Enter (internal rotation), Remove posterior arm, Roll (Gaskin). (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

Delivery of the posterior arm reduces the bisacromial diameter by approximately 2 cm and resolves the majority of shoulder dystocias that fail external manoeuvres. (Source: KEMH: Shoulder Dystocia Guideline)
Fundal pressure is contraindicated in shoulder dystocia - it worsens impaction and increases the risk of brachial plexus injury and uterine rupture. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)
GDM with macrosomia is a major risk factor; this woman met ADIPS diagnostic criteria on all three OGTT values. (Source: ADIPS: Diagnostic Criteria for GDM)
explanation.pearls: When entering the sacral hollow for posterior arm delivery, follow the curve of the sacrum posteriorly to avoid fracturing the humerus - splint the bone with your fingers along its length, never apply pressure at a single point.

stem: A 24-year-old primigravida at 36+4 weeks gestation is brought to the birth suite by ambulance following a tonic-clonic seizure at home that lasted approximately 2 minutes. She has a known diagnosis of pre-eclampsia with severe features diagnosed 3 days ago, for which she has been receiving oral labetalol 200 mg BD. She had been complaining of a severe frontal headache and epigastric pain since this morning. On arrival, she is postictal and drowsy with a GCS of 13 (E3 V4 M6). Her blood pressure is 175/115 mmHg on the left arm in the semi-recumbent position, confirmed with a repeat reading 15 minutes later. She has 3+ protein on urine dipstick and hyperreflexia with 4 beats of clonus at the ankle. IV access has been established. The obstetric and anaesthetic teams are present, and plans are being made for urgent delivery once she is stabilised. She weighs 72 kg. Her most recent bloods from 2 hours ago show: Hb 108 g/L, platelets 95 x 10^9/L, creatinine 88 micromol/L, ALT 125 U/L, urate 0.48 mmol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 175/115 mmHg (confirmed), HR 98 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 96% on room air, Temp 37.1°C, GCS 13 postictal
data_table.Examination: Postictal, drowsy, hyperreflexia, 4 beats ankle clonus, 3+ proteinuria, epigastric tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Hb 108 g/L, Platelets 95 x 10^9/L, Creatinine 88 micromol/L, ALT 125 U/L, Urate 0.48 mmol/L (2 hrs ago)
data_table.Medications: Labetalol 200 mg PO BD for 3 days (last dose 4 hours ago)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate magnesium sulphate regimen for this woman?

options.text: Magnesium sulphate 4 g IV loading dose over 20 minutes, then 1 g/hour IV infusion for 24 hours after delivery or 24 hours after the last seizure, whichever is later
options.rationale: The standard Australian eclampsia regimen is a 4 g IV loading dose over 20 minutes followed by a 1 g/hour maintenance infusion. Therapy is continued for 24 hours after delivery OR 24 hours after the last seizure, whichever occurs later. This is distinct from the pre-eclampsia prophylaxis regimen (which omits the loading dose). (Source: SOMANZ Guideline: Management of Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy; KEMH: Eclampsia Guideline)

options.text: Magnesium sulphate 4 g IV loading dose over 5 minutes, then 2 g/hour IV infusion for 24 hours postpartum
options.rationale: A 5-minute loading infusion carries a risk of hypotension and maternal collapse. The standard loading dose is infused over 20 minutes. A maintenance rate of 2 g/hour exceeds the standard Australian regimen of 1 g/hour and increases the risk of magnesium toxicity without demonstrated additional benefit. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy)

options.text: Magnesium sulphate 4 g IM loading dose into each buttock (8 g total), then 5 g IM 4-hourly for 24 hours
options.rationale: The IM-only Pritchard regimen is rarely used in contemporary Australian practice. It is painful and absorption is unreliable. The IV regimen is standard of care in Australian tertiary settings with IV access available. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy)

options.text: Magnesium sulphate 2 g IV loading dose over 20 minutes, then 1 g/hour IV infusion for 24 hours after the last seizure
options.rationale: A 2 g loading dose is insufficient for eclampsia. The standard eclampsia treatment regimen requires a 4 g loading dose. A 2 g loading dose would be appropriate for seizure prophylaxis in severe pre-eclampsia but is not adequate for treating established eclampsia. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy)

options.text: Magnesium sulphate 6 g IV loading dose over 30 minutes, then 1.5 g/hour IV infusion for 48 hours postpartum
options.rationale: A 6 g loading dose exceeds the standard 4 g recommendation and increases toxicity risk without evidence of benefit. The 48-hour duration also exceeds standard recommendations - therapy is typically continued for 24 hours after the last seizure or 24 hours after delivery, whichever is later. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: This woman has eclampsia superimposed on severe pre-eclampsia (BP 175/115, thrombocytopenia, elevated ALT, hyperreflexia with clonus). The priority is seizure control with magnesium sulphate using the correct eclampsia treatment regimen: 4 g IV loading dose over 20 minutes followed by 1 g/hour maintenance infusion. This differs from the prophylactic regimen used for severe pre-eclampsia (which omits the loading dose in some protocols). Delivery will follow maternal stabilisation.
key_points: Eclampsia is treated with magnesium sulphate: 4 g IV loading dose over 20 minutes, then 1 g/hour maintenance infusion. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy)
Magnesium therapy is continued for 24 hours after delivery OR 24 hours after the last seizure, whichever is LATER. (Source: KEMH: Eclampsia Guideline)

Magnesium toxicity monitoring includes assessment of patellar reflexes (lost at 3.5-5 mmol/L), respiratory rate, urine output, and serum magnesium levels if concerned. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy)

The platelet count of 95 x 10^9/L and elevated ALT are features of severe pre-eclampsia with end-organ involvement, which mandates urgent delivery after stabilisation. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy)
explanation.pearls: Recurrent seizures despite adequate magnesium therapy occur in approximately 10% of eclampsia cases - treat with a further 2-4 g IV bolus of magnesium sulphate and consider alternative diagnoses (e.g. intracranial haemorrhage) if seizures persist.

stem: A 31-year-old G3P2 at 39+1 weeks gestation has just delivered a 4.2 kg infant vaginally following a labour augmented with oxytocin for 14 hours. The placenta delivered spontaneously 10 minutes later. Her estimated blood loss is now 800 mL and ongoing. The uterus is boggy on palpation and the fundus is above the umbilicus. The accoucheur has commenced uterine fundal massage and the midwife is running an IV infusion of oxytocin 40 units in 1 L of normal saline at 250 mL/hour. The bladder has been emptied with a straight catheter. Despite these measures for 5 minutes, the bleeding continues and the uterus remains atonic. Her current vital signs are HR 112 bpm, BP 100/65 mmHg, RR 20. She has no known drug allergies. Her antenatal haemoglobin 2 weeks ago was 115 g/L. She has a history of asthma well-controlled with salbutamol as needed, with no exacerbations in the past year.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 112 bpm, BP 100/65 mmHg, RR 20, SpO2 98%
data_table.Examination: Uterus boggy, fundus above umbilicus, ongoing moderate-heavy vaginal bleeding, placenta appears complete on inspection
data_table.Investigations: Antenatal Hb 115 g/L (2 weeks prior), current estimated blood loss 800 mL and ongoing
data_table.Medications: Oxytocin 40 U in 1 L normal saline at 250 mL/hr IV; salbutamol PRN for asthma

lead_in: Which SINGLE uterotonic agent should be administered next?

options.text: IM syntometrine 1 mL (oxytocin 5 IU + ergometrine 500 mcg) as a single dose
options.rationale: Syntometrine contains ergometrine, which is relatively contraindicated in patients with hypertension (though this woman's current BP is not hypertensive, she is at risk of hypertension from oxytocin and ergometrine). Furthermore, syntometrine is typically used for prophylaxis (third stage) rather than treatment of established PPH. Ergometrine is a second-line agent after oxytocin for PPH treatment, but the woman's asthma is an additional relative contraindication. (Source: KEMH: Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline; Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Emergencies)

options.text: IM carboprost 250 mcg (Haemabate) every 15 minutes up to a maximum of 8 doses
options.rationale: Carboprost (prostaglandin F2-alpha) is a second-line uterotonic for PPH but is relatively contraindicated in asthma because it can cause significant bronchospasm. In a woman with known asthma, even if well-controlled, it should be used with extreme caution and is generally avoided when alternatives exist. Ergometrine would typically be tried first. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Emergencies; KEMH: Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

options.text: IV tranexamic acid 1 g slowly over 10 minutes as a single dose and IV ergometrine 250 mcg slowly
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid (TXA) is indicated in PPH and should be given within 3 hours of bleeding onset. However, TXA is an antifibrinolytic, not a uterotonic. It should be given AS WELL AS a uterotonic, not as the sole next agent. The combination here includes ergometrine which is appropriate, but the question asks for the SINGLE next agent. The priority is to give a second-line uterotonic; TXA is an adjunct. (Source: WOMAN Trial; KEMH: Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

options.text: IV ergometrine 250 mcg slowly over 1-2 minutes, repeated once after 5 minutes if needed
options.rationale: Ergometrine is the standard second-line uterotonic in PPH after oxytocin, provided there are no contraindications. The main contraindications are hypertension (current BP normal at 100/65) and cardiac disease. Asthma is a relative contraindication but is not absolute - ergometrine can cause bronchospasm, but in a well-controlled asthmatic with no recent exacerbations, the benefit of controlling life-threatening haemorrhage outweighs this risk. Carboprost would be more problematic in asthma. (Source: KEMH: Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline; Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Emergencies)

options.text: Misoprostol 800 mcg per rectum as a single dose
options.rationale: Rectal misoprostol has a slower onset of action compared to parenteral uterotonics and is not the recommended second-line agent in the acute management of PPH when IV access is available and the haemorrhage is ongoing. It may be used when other uterotonics are contraindicated or unavailable, but ergometrine is the appropriate next step here. (Source: KEMH: Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

explanation.summary: This woman has a primary PPH due to uterine atony (boggy uterus, fundus above umbilicus, EBL 800 mL and rising). First-line measures - fundal massage, bladder emptying, and IV oxytocin infusion - are in progress but bleeding continues. The next step is a second-line uterotonic. Ergometrine 250 mcg IV is the appropriate choice; her BP is normal (no hypertensive contraindication) and her asthma is well-controlled (relative, not absolute, contraindication). Carboprost would be more dangerous in this asthmatic patient. TXA should be given alongside, not instead of, a uterotonic.
key_points: Second-line uterotonic for PPH when oxytocin has failed is IV ergometrine 250 mcg, repeated once after 5 minutes if needed (maximum 500 mcg). (Source: KEMH: Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

Contraindications to ergometrine: hypertension, pre-eclampsia, cardiac disease, and severe asthma with recent exacerbation. Well-controlled asthma is a relative, not absolute, contraindication. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Emergencies)
Tranexamic acid 1 g IV should be given within 3 hours of PPH onset but is an ADJUNCT to, not a replacement for, uterotonic therapy. (Source: WOMAN Trial; KEMH: Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
Carboprost is relatively contraindicated in asthma because prostaglandin F2-alpha causes bronchoconstriction. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Emergencies)
explanation.pearls: When PPH is recognised, call for help, initiate a PPH emergency, and remember the '4 Ts' - Tone (atony - most common), Trauma, Tissue (retained products), Thrombin (coagulopathy). Atony alone accounts for approximately 70% of PPH cases.

subtopic: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes – Management at 28 weeks
stem: A 27-year-old G2P1 at 28+3 weeks gestation presents to the emergency department reporting a sudden gush of clear fluid from her vagina approximately 2 hours ago while sitting at her desk. She has since noted ongoing trickling of fluid. She reports no pain, no contractions, and normal foetal movements. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated to date with a normal morphology scan at 20 weeks. On examination, her temperature is 37.9°C, HR 92 bpm, BP 110/68 mmHg. The abdomen is soft and non-tender, fundal height is 28 cm. On speculum examination, a pool of clear fluid is seen in the posterior fornix with a positive nitrazine test. The cervix is closed and long on visual inspection. A foetal fibronectin swab has been taken (result pending) and a high vaginal swab has been sent for microscopy and culture. Cardiotocography shows a baseline of 150 bpm with moderate variability, no decelerations, and irregular tightenings every 10-12 minutes. Her most recent cervical length at 20 weeks was 42 mm. She received a full course of betamethasone for threatened preterm labour at 26 weeks (which resolved spontaneously).

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.9°C, HR 92 bpm, BP 110/68 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 99%
data_table.Examination: Speculum: pooling of clear fluid, positive nitrazine, cervix closed and long; fundal height 28 cm
data_table.Investigations: CTG baseline 150 bpm, moderate variability, no decelerations, tightenings q10-12 min; cervical length 42 mm at 20 wks
data_table.Medications: Betamethasone course completed at 26 weeks (2 doses 24 hrs apart)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Admit for inpatient monitoring, administer a rescue course of betamethasone (11.4 mg IM, two doses 24 hours apart), commence oral azithromycin 1 g stat and ampicillin 2 g IV 6-hourly for 48 hours, then amoxicillin 250 mg PO 8-hourly for 5 days
options.rationale: A rescue course of corticosteroids is indicated when the prior course was given more than 7 days ago and delivery is anticipated within the next 7 days. Here the prior course was at 26 weeks, now 28+3 weeks, which is more than 7 days, so a rescue course is indicated. However, the temperature of 37.9°C is a red flag for possible chorioamnionitis; the question becomes whether delivery rather than conservative management is indicated. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline; KEMH: PPROM Guideline)

options.text: Induction of labour with intravenous oxytocin, as PPROM at 28+3 weeks with a temperature of 37.9°C mandates delivery within 24 hours
options.rationale: A single temperature of 37.9°C in an otherwise well woman with no other signs of chorioamnionitis (no uterine tenderness, normal foetal heart rate pattern, no purulent discharge) does not mandate immediate delivery. A temperature of 37.9°C is a borderline finding; serial observations and inflammatory markers are required to diagnose chorioamnionitis. This temperature alone is not a delivery trigger. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline; KEMH: PPROM Guideline)

options.text: Admit for conservative management with 4-hourly observations (temperature, HR, BP), twice-weekly CTG, weekly bloods (WCC, CRP), administer a rescue course of betamethasone, commence oral erythromycin 250 mg QID for 10 days, and plan delivery at 34+0 weeks if undelivered
options.rationale: This woman is at 28+3 weeks, PPROM has been confirmed, she is clinically stable with no clear signs of chorioamnionitis. The standard approach is conservative management with close surveillance for chorioamnionitis (4-hourly temp, HR, twice-weekly CTG, weekly inflammatory markers). A rescue course of betamethasone is indicated (prior course >7 days ago). Erythromycin 250 mg QID for 10 days is the recommended latency antibiotic in Australian practice. Delivery is planned at 34+0 weeks if undelivered. The borderline temperature of 37.9°C warrants close observation but is not yet an indication for delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline; KEMH: PPROM Guideline)

options.text: Administer a single rescue dose of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM, observe for 4 hours, and discharge home with strict return precautions if the temperature normalises and there are no signs of chorioamnionitis
options.rationale: Outpatient management of PPROM is not recommended. The risk of ascending infection, cord prolapse, and placental abruption requires inpatient monitoring with regular observations. Discharge home, even with strict return precautions, is not standard care for PPROM at any gestation. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline)

options.text: Admit for inpatient monitoring, administer a rescue course of betamethasone, commence IV ampicillin 2 g 6-hourly and oral clarithromycin 500 mg BD for 10 days, and plan delivery at 37+0 weeks if undelivered
options.rationale: This plan uses ampicillin/clarithromycin instead of the Australian-recommended erythromycin regimen (based on the ORACLE trial, erythromycin alone is recommended; co-amoxiclav is associated with increased NEC risk). Additionally, delivery at 37+0 weeks is too conservative - PPROM is typically delivered by 34+0 weeks in Australian practice (the risk of chorioamnionitis after 34 weeks begins to outweigh the prematurity risk). (Source: KEMH: PPROM Guideline; RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline)

explanation.summary: This woman at 28+3 weeks with confirmed PPROM should be managed conservatively with inpatient monitoring for chorioamnionitis, rescue corticosteroids, and latency antibiotics (erythromycin). The borderline temperature of 37.9°C warrants close observation but is not a delivery indication in isolation. Delivery is planned at 34+0 weeks. The ORACLE trial demonstrated that erythromycin prolongs latency and reduces neonatal morbidity; co-amoxiclav is avoided due to increased NEC risk.
key_points: Conservative management of PPROM <34 weeks: inpatient monitoring, 4-hourly obs (temp, HR), twice-weekly CTG, weekly WCC/CRP. (Source: KEMH: PPROM Guideline)
Rescue course of betamethasone is indicated if >7 days since last course and delivery anticipated <7 days. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline)
Latency antibiotics: erythromycin 250 mg QID for 10 days (ORACLE trial). Co-amoxiclav is avoided due to increased NEC risk. (Source: ORACLE Trial; KEMH: PPROM Guideline)
Delivery is planned at 34+0 weeks for PPROM; chorioamnionitis, non-reassuring foetal status, or labour mandate earlier delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline)
explanation.pearls: A single borderline temperature does not diagnose chorioamnionitis - look for the triad of maternal fever (>38.0°C) PLUS two or more of: maternal tachycardia, foetal tachycardia, uterine tenderness, purulent or offensive vaginal discharge, or rising WCC/CRP.

stem: A 33-year-old G2P1 at 39+0 weeks presents in spontaneous labour at 4 cm dilated. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated. Her GBS status is unknown - the screening swab at 36 weeks was not performed because she was travelling interstate. Her previous pregnancy 4 years ago was uncomplicated, and she delivered vaginally at term. Her first infant did not develop GBS disease. On direct questioning, she reports no symptoms of a urinary tract infection during this pregnancy and no history of a positive GBS swab. Her temperature is 36.7°C, and observations are normal. The membranes are intact at present. The CTG is reassuring with a baseline of 140 bpm and normal variability. The woman has no known drug allergies.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.7°C, HR 80 bpm, BP 115/72 mmHg, RR 16
data_table.Examination: Cervix 4 cm dilated, membranes intact, cephalic presentation, station -1
data_table.Investigations: CTG baseline 140 bpm, normal variability, accelerations present; GBS swab at 36 weeks: NOT DONE
data_table.Medications: Nil regular, no known drug allergies

lead_in: What is the most appropriate intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis plan?

options.text: No intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis is required; treat only if the infant develops clinical signs of sepsis
options.rationale: With an unknown GBS status and the presence of risk factors, prophylaxis should be offered. Simply waiting for infant signs is not appropriate - the purpose of IAP is to prevent early-onset GBS disease before clinical signs develop. (Source: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy; KEMH: GBS Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy

options.text: Benzylpenicillin 1.2 g IV loading dose, then 600 mg IV 4-hourly until delivery, commencing immediately as she is in labour with unknown GBS status
options.rationale: IAP is not indicated for ALL women with unknown GBS status in labour. It is indicated when the GBS status is unknown AND there are clinical risk factors: preterm labour <37 weeks, ROM ≥18 hours, maternal temperature ≥38.0°C, previous infant with GBS disease, or GBS bacteriuria in current pregnancy. This woman has none of these risk factors, so IAP is not yet indicated. (Source: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy

options.text: Take a low vaginal swab for GBS culture and PCR now, and commence benzylpenicillin 1.2 g IV loading dose only if the swab returns positive or membranes rupture
options.rationale: Taking a swab in labour is not standard practice - the result will not be available in time to guide IAP. The decision to give IAP should be based on the clinical risk factors at the time of labour, not on a swab taken during labour. (Source: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy

options.text: Monitor for the development of clinical risk factors - if membranes rupture for ≥18 hours or maternal temperature reaches ≥38.0°C, commence benzylpenicillin 1.2 g IV loading dose then 600 mg IV 4-hourly
options.rationale: For women with unknown GBS status at term, IAP is indicated ONLY if clinical risk factors develop: ROM ≥18 hours, maternal temperature ≥38.0°C, preterm labour <37 weeks, GBS bacteriuria in current pregnancy, or previous infant with GBS disease. This woman currently has none of these, so the plan is expectant management with monitoring for risk factors. (Source: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy; KEMH: GBS Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy

options.text: Cefazolin 2 g IV loading dose then 1 g IV 8-hourly as the preferred antibiotic for unknown GBS status with no risk factors
options.rationale: Cefazolin is an alternative to penicillin for GBS IAP in the setting of non-severe penicillin allergy. It is not indicated for unknown GBS status without risk factors, and it is not indicated if penicillin can be used. Additionally, this woman has no drug allergies, so penicillin is the preferred agent if IAP becomes indicated. (Source: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy

explanation.summary: This term woman with unknown GBS status and no clinical risk factors does not require immediate IAP. The standard Australian approach is to monitor for the development of risk factors during labour: ROM ≥18 hours, intrapartum fever ≥38.0°C, or preterm birth. If none develop, IAP is not indicated. This differs from some international approaches that recommend universal IAP for unknown GBS status, but Australian guidance uses a risk-factor-based approach for women whose screening status is unknown.
key_points: IAP is indicated for known GBS-positive women AND for unknown-GBS women with clinical risk factors: ROM ≥18 hrs, temp ≥38.0°C, <37 wks, GBS bacteriuria, previous infant with GBS disease. (Source: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy)
For unknown GBS status at term with NO risk factors, IAP is not required. (Source: KEMH: GBS Guideline)
The standard IAP regimen is benzylpenicillin 1.2 g IV loading dose then 600 mg IV 4-hourly until delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy)
Clindamycin is used for women with severe penicillin allergy only if GBS sensitivities are known; vancomycin is used if sensitivities are unknown. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)
explanation.pearls: IAP is considered adequate if the loading dose is given at least 4 hours before delivery. Document the time of first dose clearly to guide neonatal management.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy

subtopic: Foetal growth restriction – Timing of delivery
stem: A 29-year-old primigravida at 29+5 weeks gestation is being managed as an inpatient for early-onset foetal growth restriction. Her pregnancy is otherwise uncomplicated with a normal morphology scan at 20 weeks. She has no significant past medical history and is a non-smoker. Serial growth ultrasounds have shown an estimated foetal weight consistently below the 3rd centile since 24 weeks with a falling growth velocity. Umbilical artery Doppler studies today show absent end-diastolic flow. Previous Dopplers 2 weeks ago showed a raised umbilical artery pulsatility index (>95th centile) but positive end-diastolic flow. Middle cerebral artery Doppler today shows a low pulsatility index consistent with brain-sparing. The ductus venosus Doppler shows a positive a-wave. The amniotic fluid index is 7.5 cm (borderline oligohydramnios). CTG today shows a baseline of 145 bpm with reduced variability (3-5 bpm) and occasional spontaneous accelerations, but no decelerations. The woman has received a full course of betamethasone (two doses 24 hours apart) completed yesterday. Her blood pressure is 120/78 mmHg, and urinalysis shows no protein. She is on twice-daily CTG monitoring and has been counselled that delivery is likely within the next 48 hours. The neonatal team has reviewed her. Magnesium sulphate for foetal neuroprotection has not yet been commenced.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 120/78 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, RR 16, Temp 36.6°C
data_table.Examination: No protein on urinalysis, fundal height 25 cm (consistent with FGR)
data_table.Investigations: UA Doppler: absent end-diastolic flow (AEDF); MCA PI low (brain-sparing); DV a-wave positive; AFI 7.5 cm; CTG: baseline 145 bpm, reduced variability (3-5 bpm), no decelerations; EFW <3rd centile

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence IV magnesium sulphate 4 g loading dose for foetal neuroprotection and plan for delivery within 24 hours given the AEDF and reduced CTG variability
options.rationale: AEDF with reduced CTG variability indicates foetal compromise that warrants expedited delivery. However, delivery is typically planned within 24-48 hours rather than urgently (hours), provided the ductus venosus a-wave remains positive and CTG shows no decelerations. Immediate delivery (within hours) is indicated when AEDF is accompanied by abnormal DV Doppler (absent/reversed a-wave) or recurrent decelerations. Magnesium sulphate for neuroprotection should be commenced, but the timing of delivery for this specific combination is 24-48 hours. (Source: RANZCOG: Foetal Growth Restriction Guideline; KEMH: FGR Guideline)

options.text: Commence IV magnesium sulphate 4 g loading dose over 20 minutes followed by 1 g/hour maintenance, and plan delivery within 24-48 hours with continuous intrapartum CTG monitoring
options.rationale: In FGR at 29+5 weeks with AEDF, brain-sparing, borderline AFI, and reduced CTG variability (but no decelerations and DV a-wave positive), delivery is indicated within 24-48 hours. Magnesium sulphate for foetal neuroprotection (4 g IV loading dose over 20 minutes, then 1 g/hour maintenance) should be commenced, given the gestation is <30 weeks and delivery is imminent. The positive DV a-wave and absence of CTG decelerations allow for this timeframe without requiring immediate emergency delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Foetal Growth Restriction Guideline; KEMH: FGR Guideline)

options.text: Perform immediate caesarean section as the AEDF and reduced CTG variability indicate acute-on-chronic foetal compromise requiring delivery within 2-4 hours
options.rationale: AEDF with reduced variability does NOT meet criteria for immediate delivery (Category 1 caesarean). Immediate delivery is indicated for recurrent decelerations, reversed end-diastolic flow, or abnormal ductus venosus Doppler (absent/reversed a-wave). In the presence of a positive DV a-wave and no decelerations, delivery can be planned within 24-48 hours with close monitoring. (Source: RANZCOG: Foetal Growth Restriction Guideline)

options.text: Perform a repeat CTG in 12 hours and only plan delivery if the CTG deteriorates to show recurrent decelerations or the ductus venosus a-wave becomes absent or reversed
options.rationale: AEDF with reduced CTG variability is an indication for delivery, not for continued observation. Expectant management beyond 48 hours is not appropriate once these features are present. Delivery should be planned within 24-48 hours, not deferred until further deterioration. (Source: RANZCOG: Foetal Growth Restriction Guideline)

options.text: Administer a rescue course of betamethasone (two further doses 24 hours apart) and plan for delivery at 32+0 weeks, repeating umbilical artery Dopplers twice weekly
options.rationale: A rescue course of corticosteroids is indicated if >7 days have elapsed since the previous course. This course was completed YESTERDAY, so a rescue course is not appropriate at this point. Furthermore, AEDF with reduced CTG variability mandates delivery within 24-48 hours, not at 32+0 weeks. Continued expectant management with twice-weekly Dopplers is not safe. (Source: RANZCOG: Foetal Growth Restriction Guideline)

explanation.summary: This woman at 29+5 weeks has FGR with AEDF, brain-sparing, borderline AFI, and reduced CTG variability. The combination warrants delivery within 24-48 hours. The positive DV a-wave and absence of CTG decelerations allow this window rather than mandating immediate delivery. Magnesium sulphate for foetal neuroprotection (<30 weeks, delivery imminent) should be commenced. Corticosteroids have been given and do not need repeating. The key decision is timing: AEDF + reduced variability = deliver within 24-48 hours; AEDF + abnormal DV or decelerations = deliver within hours.
key_points: AEDF on UA Doppler in FGR indicates delivery within 24-48 hours if CTG is non-reassuring but without decelerations and DV a-wave remains positive. (Source: RANZCOG: Foetal Growth Restriction Guideline)

Magnesium sulphate 4 g IV loading dose over 20 minutes for foetal neuroprotection is indicated when delivery is planned <30 weeks gestation. (Source: KEMH: Magnesium Sulphate for Neuroprotection Guideline)

Immediate delivery (Category 1 CS) is indicated for AEDF with recurrent CTG decelerations, reversed EDF, or abnormal DV Doppler (absent/reversed a-wave). (Source: RANZCOG: Foetal Growth Restriction Guideline)
A rescue course of corticosteroids is indicated if >7 days since the last course AND delivery is anticipated within the next 7 days. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroids Guideline)
explanation.pearls: The ductus venosus a-wave is the last Doppler parameter to become abnormal in FGR deterioration - a positive a-wave is reassuring but does not exclude significant compromise. It is the 'canary in the coal mine' for foetal acid-base status.

sources.label: KEMH: Magnesium Sulphate for Neuroprotection Guideline

stem: A 30-year-old G3P1 at 34+2 weeks gestation presents with a sudden small amount of dark red vaginal bleeding that began immediately after she felt a 'popping' sensation and a gush of fluid 30 minutes ago. She reports no pain. Her pregnancy was complicated by a low-lying placenta noted at the 20-week morphology scan, which was reported to have cleared the os by the 32-week follow-up scan. A velamentous cord insertion was noted on the 32-week scan but vasa praevia was not specifically reported. On arrival, she is haemodynamically stable with HR 88 bpm and BP 120/75 mmHg. Speculum examination shows dark blood in the vagina with ongoing oozing. A bedside cardiotocograph is performed immediately and shows a foetal heart rate baseline of 120 bpm, reduced variability, and a sinusoidal-like pattern with regular shallow oscillations. There are no accelerations and no decelerations. The estimated blood loss is approximately 100 mL. The cervix is not examined digitally.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal HR 88 bpm, BP 120/75 mmHg, RR 18, Temp 36.5°C, SpO2 98%
data_table.Examination: Speculum: dark blood in vagina, ongoing oozing; digital examination deferred; abdomen soft non-tender
data_table.Investigations: CTG: baseline 120 bpm, reduced variability, sinusoidal-like pattern; 32-week scan: low-lying placenta cleared, velamentous cord insertion noted; EBL ~100 mL

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis underlying the CTG pattern?

options.text: Placental abruption causing foetal hypoxia and compensatory foetal heart rate pattern
options.rationale: Placental abruption typically presents with PAINFUL bleeding, uterine tenderness, and a hypertonic uterus. This woman has painless bleeding after ROM, a known velamentous cord insertion, and a sinusoidal CTG pattern - this is more consistent with vasa praevia with foetal haemorrhage. The sinusoidal pattern reflects severe foetal anaemia. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline; KEMH: Vasa Praevia Guideline)

options.text: Placenta praevia with a recurrent low-lying placenta causing marginal sinus bleeding and foetal bradycardia
options.rationale: The placenta was confirmed to have cleared the os at the 32-week scan, making placenta praevia unlikely. Furthermore, placenta praevia bleeding is maternal blood and would not directly cause a sinusoidal CTG pattern unless there was massive maternal haemorrhage causing hypotension, which is not present here (EBL 100 mL, maternal HR and BP stable). (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

options.text: Vasa praevia with rupture of a foetal vessel traversing the membranes overlying the internal os
options.rationale: The triad of painless vaginal bleeding following membrane rupture, a known velamentous cord insertion, and a sinusoidal CTG pattern is pathognomonic for vasa praevia with foetal vessel rupture. The blood loss is small (100 mL) because it represents foetal haemorrhage - a foetus has a total blood volume of only approximately 80 mL/kg, so even 100 mL is catastrophic. The sinusoidal CTG pattern reflects severe foetal anaemia. (Source: RANZCOG: Vasa Praevia Guideline; KEMH: Vasa Praevia Guideline)

options.text: Uterine rupture with extrusion of the foetus into the peritoneal cavity and severe foetal distress
options.rationale: Uterine rupture presents with acute severe abdominal pain, loss of the presenting part, vaginal bleeding, and maternal haemodynamic instability. This woman has no pain and is haemodynamically stable with a stable uterus. The scenario is not consistent with uterine rupture. (Source: RANZCOG: Uterine Rupture Guideline)

options.text: Foetal-maternal haemorrhage due to a placental lake rupture during membrane rupture
options.rationale: Foetal-maternal haemorrhage (FMH) is a recognised complication but typically presents with persistent foetal bradycardia or a sinusoidal pattern WITHOUT visible vaginal bleeding (the foetal blood enters the maternal circulation). Visible vaginal bleeding after ROM in the context of velamentous cord insertion is far more consistent with vasa praevia. Additionally, FMH would not directly present with visible vaginal bleeding. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

explanation.summary: This woman has the classic presentation of vasa praevia: painless vaginal bleeding immediately following membrane rupture, a known velamentous cord insertion (a major risk factor), and a sinusoidal CTG pattern indicating severe foetal anaemia. The blood loss is small because it represents foetal, not maternal, haemorrhage - a foetus at 34 weeks has a total blood volume of only approximately 250 mL, so even 100 mL is life-threatening. Immediate caesarean delivery is indicated.
key_points: Vasa praevia occurs when foetal vessels run across the internal os within the membranes, at risk of rupture at ROM. (Source: RANZCOG: Vasa Praevia Guideline)
A sinusoidal CTG pattern is a classic sign of severe foetal anaemia and requires immediate delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance)

The key risk factor is velamentous cord insertion, especially with a low-lying placenta in the second trimester; these pregnancies should have a targeted scan for vasa praevia. (Source: KEMH: Vasa Praevia Guideline)
Management is immediate caesarean section - foetal survival depends on prompt recognition and delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Vasa Praevia Guideline)
explanation.pearls: A sinusoidal pattern on CTG is an obstetric 'code blue' - call for immediate help, prepare for emergency CS, and alert neonatal team for foetal resuscitation.

subtopic: Early pregnancy – Ectopic pregnancy and methotrexate criteria
stem: A 28-year-old G3P1 at approximately 6+2 weeks gestation by LMP presents to the early pregnancy assessment service with 4 days of mild left iliac fossa pain and spotting. She has no heavy bleeding, no shoulder tip pain, and no dizziness. Her past history includes a previous uncomplicated vaginal delivery at term and a first-trimester miscarriage managed expectantly. She is haemodynamically stable with HR 78 bpm and BP 110/68 mmHg. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an empty uterus with an endometrial thickness of 8 mm. The left adnexa demonstrates a 2.4 cm mass with a hyperechoic ring but no visible yolk sac or foetal pole. There is no free fluid in the pouch of Douglas. Her serum β-hCG is 2100 IU/L, and a repeat 48 hours later is 2850 IU/L (a 36% rise). Her full blood count, renal function, and liver function tests are normal. She has no contraindications to methotrexate and has been counselled about both medical and surgical management options.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78 bpm, BP 110/68 mmHg, RR 16, Temp 36.4°C
data_table.Examination: Mild left adnexal tenderness, no cervical motion tenderness, no peritoneal signs
data_table.Investigations: TVUS: empty uterus, left adnexal mass 2.4 cm with hyperechoic ring, no yolk sac/foetal pole, no free fluid; β-hCG 2100 → 2850 (48 hr, 36% rise); FBC, UEC, LFTs normal

lead_in: Which of the following findings would make this woman UNSUITABLE for medical management with single-dose methotrexate?

options.text: An adnexal mass measuring 2.4 cm on transvaginal ultrasound
options.rationale: An adnexal mass size less than 3.5–4 cm is a criterion FOR medical management. This mass at 2.4 cm is well within the acceptable size limit. (Source: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy; KEMH: Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy

options.text: A serum β-hCG of 2850 IU/L with a suboptimal rise of 36% over 48 hours
options.rationale: A β-hCG level below 5000 IU/L is a criterion for medical management with methotrexate. The suboptimal rise confirms a non-viable pregnancy (and supports ectopic over a failed intrauterine pregnancy), but it does not preclude methotrexate use. (Source: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy

options.text: The presence of a hyperechoic ring around the adnexal mass without a visible yolk sac
options.rationale: This is the classic ultrasound appearance of a tubal ectopic pregnancy (the 'tubal ring' or 'bagel sign'). The absence of a yolk sac or foetal pole is common and does not affect suitability for methotrexate. In fact, the absence of cardiac activity is a positive predictor of success. (Source: KEMH: Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: A normal platelet count, normal serum creatinine, and normal liver transaminases
options.rationale: Normal renal, hepatic, and haematological function are prerequisites for safe methotrexate administration - they are required, not exclusionary. Methotrexate is excreted renally and metabolised by the liver; normal function is essential. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Ectopic Pregnancy; AMH: Methotrexate)

options.text: The presence of embryonic cardiac activity within the ectopic gestation on ultrasound
options.rationale: The presence of foetal cardiac activity is a contraindication to medical management with methotrexate due to a significantly higher failure rate. Cardiac activity indicates a more established ectopic gestation that is less likely to respond to methotrexate, and surgical management (salpingectomy) is generally recommended. (Source: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy; KEMH: Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy

explanation.summary: This woman has a probable tubal ectopic pregnancy based on empty uterus, adnexal mass with tubal ring sign, and suboptimal β-hCG rise. She meets most criteria for single-dose methotrexate (haemodynamic stability, adnexal mass <3.5 cm, β-hCG <5000 IU/L, no free fluid, normal organ function, no contraindications). However, foetal cardiac activity is a known contraindication due to high failure rate with medical management. The question tests recognition of exclusion criteria.
key_points: Criteria for methotrexate therapy: haemodynamic stability, unruptured ectopic, adnexal mass <3.5-4 cm, β-hCG <5000 IU/L, no foetal cardiac activity, no free fluid, and normal renal/hepatic/haematological function. (Source: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy)
Presence of foetal cardiac activity is an absolute contraindication to methotrexate due to high failure rates; surgical management is indicated. (Source: KEMH: Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline)

Suboptimal β-hCG rise (less than 66% in 48 hours) supports a non-viable pregnancy and is often seen in ectopic gestation; it does not preclude medical therapy. (Source: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy)
Single-dose methotrexate 50 mg/m² IM is the standard regimen; follow-up β-hCG at days 4 and 7 to confirm ≥15% decline between days 4 and 7. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Ectopic Pregnancy)
explanation.pearls: The five contraindications to methotrexate for ectopic pregnancy can be remembered as 'HEART': Haemodynamic instability, Embryonic cardiac activity, Adnexal mass >4 cm, Renal/hepatic dysfunction, Too-high β-hCG (>5000).

sources.label: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy

subtopic: Induction of labour – Bishop score and cervical ripening
stem: A 34-year-old primigravida at 41+3 weeks gestation presents for a scheduled induction of labour for post-dates pregnancy. She has been well throughout the pregnancy with normal foetal movements, a reactive CTG, and an amniotic fluid index of 10 cm on yesterday's ultrasound. She has no significant past medical history. On vaginal examination, the cervix is posterior, 2 cm long, firm in consistency, closed at the external os, and the presenting part is at station -3. Her Bishop score is calculated as 2. The CTG on admission is normal with a baseline of 140 bpm and moderate variability. She is counselled about the need for cervical ripening prior to oxytocin induction. She has no contraindications to prostaglandin use (no previous uterine surgery, no severe asthma, no known allergy).

data_table.Vital signs: HR 76 bpm, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 16, Temp 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Cervix posterior, 2 cm long, firm, closed, station -3; Bishop score = 2
data_table.Investigations: CTG baseline 140 bpm, moderate variability, reactive; AFI 10 cm; no contraindications to prostaglandins

lead_in: What is the most appropriate cervical ripening agent and initial dose?

options.text: Dinoprostone (PGE2) 3 mg vaginal tablet inserted into the posterior fornix, repeated after 6 hours if needed up to a maximum of two doses in 24 hours
options.rationale: This describes dinoprostone 3 mg vaginal tablet (Prostin E2) but with an incorrect administration schedule. In Australian practice, dinoprostone vaginal tablets are typically 3 mg inserted 6-hourly up to a maximum of 6 mg (two doses) in 24 hours. The more common agent for primigravidae with an unfavourable cervix is dinoprostone 2 mg gel, which is administered 6-hourly. (Source: KEMH: Induction of Labour Guideline; Therapeutic Guidelines: Induction of Labour)

options.text: Dinoprostone (PGE2) 2 mg vaginal gel inserted into the posterior fornix, repeated after 6 hours if needed up to a maximum of two doses in 24 hours
options.rationale: Dinoprostone 2 mg vaginal gel (Prostin E2) is the standard cervical ripening agent for primigravidae with an unfavourable cervix (Bishop score ≤6) in Australian practice. It is administered into the posterior fornix and can be repeated after 6 hours if the Bishop score remains unfavourable, to a maximum of two doses (4 mg total) in 24 hours. A CTG is performed after administration and prior to each repeat dose. (Source: KEMH: Induction of Labour Guideline; Therapeutic Guidelines: Induction of Labour)

options.text: Misoprostol (PGE1) 25 mcg vaginal tablet inserted into the posterior fornix, repeated after 4 hours if needed
options.rationale: Misoprostol is used for cervical ripening, but 25 mcg vaginally is a common dose for IOL; however, in Australian practice, misoprostol for IOL is typically reserved for specific settings and is used orally or vaginally in lower doses. The standard first-line agent for an unfavourable cervix in primigravidae at term is dinoprostone gel. Misoprostol may be considered, but dinoprostone is the more established agent. (Source: KEMH: Induction of Labour Guideline)

options.text: Foley catheter balloon inserted through the cervix and inflated with 30 mL sterile water, left in situ for 12 hours
options.rationale: Mechanical cervical ripening with a Foley catheter is an option for cervical ripening, but it is not the most appropriate first-line agent for a primigravida with no contraindications to prostaglandins. Pharmacological ripening with dinoprostone is preferred in this low-risk post-dates primigravida. Foley catheter may be used if prostaglandins are contraindicated (e.g., previous caesarean, severe asthma). (Source: KEMH: Induction of Labour Guideline)

options.text: Oxytocin IV infusion starting at 2 mU/min and titrated every 30 minutes according to contractions without prior cervical ripening
options.rationale: Oxytocin is less effective when the cervix is unfavourable (Bishop score ≤6). Cervical ripening with a prostaglandin or mechanical method should precede oxytocin induction in women with an unfavourable cervix. Starting oxytocin directly is associated with higher induction failure and caesarean section rates. (Source: RANZCOG: Induction of Labour Guideline)

explanation.summary: This primigravida at 41+3 weeks has an unfavourable cervix (Bishop score 2) and requires cervical ripening before oxytocin. The standard of care in Australia is dinoprostone 2 mg vaginal gel inserted into the posterior fornix, repeated after 6 hours if needed (maximum two doses in 24 hours). CTG monitoring after administration is standard. This is safe in the absence of contraindications to prostaglandins (no previous uterine scar, no severe asthma).
key_points: A Bishop score ≤6 indicates an unfavourable cervix requiring cervical ripening prior to oxytocin induction. (Source: RANZCOG: Induction of Labour Guideline)

Dinoprostone 2 mg vaginal gel (Prostin E2) is the first-line pharmacological agent for cervical ripening in Australian practice for low-risk nulliparous women. (Source: KEMH: Induction of Labour Guideline)

The maximum total dose is 4 mg (two doses of 2 mg gel) in 24 hours. CTG monitoring 30-60 minutes after administration and prior to each repeat dose. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Induction of Labour)
Mechanical methods (Foley catheter) are preferred when prostaglandins are contraindicated (e.g., previous caesarean section, severe asthma). (Source: KEMH: Induction of Labour Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Bishop score components: Cervical Dilation (0-3), Effacement/Length (0-3), Station (-3 to +2 mapped 0-3), Consistency (firm/medium/soft 0-2), Position (posterior/mid/anterior 0-2). A score of 2 means you have a long, firm, closed, posterior cervix - the least favourable.

stem: A 32-year-old nulligravid woman presents to the emergency department with progressive abdominal distension, bloating, and reduced urine output over the past 3 days. She underwent oocyte retrieval for IVF 8 days ago after ovarian stimulation with a GnRH antagonist protocol and a trigger injection of hCG. She reports no vomiting but has mild dyspnoea on exertion. On examination, her abdomen is distended, tense, and diffusely tender without peritonism. A fluid thrill is present. Her weight today is 68 kg, up from a pre-cycle weight of 62 kg. Her HR is 102 bpm, BP 95/60 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 96% on room air. Urine output over the past 6 hours has been 120 mL (20 mL/hr). A transvaginal ultrasound shows bilateral enlarged ovaries measuring 10 cm and 11 cm with multiple cysts, and a moderate amount of ascites. Her haemoglobin is 165 g/L, haematocrit 0.49, WCC 16.5 x 10^9/L, serum creatinine 105 micromol/L (pre-cycle 62 micromol/L), ALT 55 U/L. Coagulation profile is normal.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 102 bpm, BP 95/60 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 96% RA, Temp 36.8°C
data_table.Examination: Abdomen distended, tense, diffuse tenderness, fluid thrill present; weight gain 6 kg over 8 days
data_table.Investigations: TVUS: ovaries 10 cm and 11 cm, multiple cysts, moderate ascites; Hb 165 g/L, Hct 0.49, WCC 16.5, Creatinine 105 (from 62), ALT 55, Coags normal; urine output 20 mL/hr
data_table.Medications: IVF stimulation cycle with GnRH antagonist protocol; hCG trigger 8 days ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate NEXT step in management?

options.text: Commence IV fluid resuscitation with a balanced crystalloid at 1-2 mL/kg/hr, insert an indwelling catheter for strict fluid balance, commence prophylactic dose low molecular weight heparin, and admit under the gynaecology team for daily assessment
options.rationale: While this describes some elements of supportive care for OHSS, the presence of oliguria (20 mL/hr) despite 6 kg weight gain, haemoconcentration (Hb 165, Hct 0.49), and a tense abdomen with moderate ascites indicate moderate-to-severe OHSS that requires more aggressive intervention. Prophylactic LMWH is not sufficient - therapeutic anticoagulation is not yet indicated, but the haemoconcentration and oliguria require careful fluid management with consideration of paracentesis. The plan should also include thromboprophylaxis, but the question asks for the most appropriate next step, which is paracentesis for symptomatic relief and to improve renal perfusion. (Source: RANZCOG: Ovarian Hyperstimulation Syndrome; KEMH: OHSS Guideline)

options.text: Perform an ultrasound-guided paracentesis to drain ascites under strict aseptic technique, commence IV fluid resuscitation cautiously, insert an indwelling catheter, and administer prophylactic dose low molecular weight heparin
options.rationale: This woman has moderate-to-severe OHSS with significant ascites causing abdominal distension, dyspnoea, and likely contributing to renal impairment (oliguria, elevated creatinine) through abdominal compartment syndrome. Paracentesis is indicated for symptomatic relief and to improve renal perfusion. IV fluids should be given cautiously to avoid worsening third-space fluid accumulation, and thromboprophylaxis with LMWH is mandatory given the hypercoagulable state of OHSS. (Source: RANZCOG: Ovarian Hyperstimulation Syndrome; KEMH: OHSS Guideline)

options.text: Administer therapeutic dose low molecular weight heparin, as the haemoconcentration and oliguria indicate severe OHSS with high thrombotic risk requiring full anticoagulation
options.rationale: While OHSS carries a significant thrombotic risk, therapeutic anticoagulation is not routinely indicated unless there is clinical evidence of thromboembolism. Prophylactic dose LMWH is standard for moderate-severe OHSS. Therapeutic dosing would be an over-escalation and carries a bleeding risk in the presence of recent oocyte retrieval (which is a procedure with risk of ovarian bleeding). (Source: RANZCOG: Ovarian Hyperstimulation Syndrome)

options.text: Discharge home on oral cabergoline 0.5 mg daily for 8 days, with strict instructions for daily weight monitoring, fluid intake of 3 L/day, and return if symptoms worsen
options.rationale: Cabergoline is a dopamine agonist used as a preventive measure for OHSS in high-risk patients, but it is not a treatment for established moderate-severe OHSS with oliguria and ascites. This woman requires inpatient management with paracentesis, cautious IV fluids, and thromboprophylaxis. Discharge home is unsafe at this stage. (Source: RANZCOG: Ovarian Hyperstimulation Syndrome)

options.text: Perform a diagnostic laparoscopy to exclude ovarian torsion, given the bilateral adnexal enlargement and sudden onset of abdominal distension and pain
options.rationale: Ovarian torsion is a differential in OHSS, but the bilateral enlargement, ascites, and systemic signs (haemoconcentration, renal impairment) are classic for OHSS. There is no acute severe unilateral pain, no Doppler evidence of absent ovarian flow, and surgical intervention would be high risk in the context of severe OHSS with coagulopathy risk. Paracentesis is the priority. (Source: KEMH: OHSS Guideline)

explanation.summary: This woman has moderate-to-severe early-onset OHSS post-IVF, characterised by haemoconcentration (Hb 165, Hct 0.49), renal impairment (creatinine rise, oliguria), ascites, and enlarged ovaries. The key interventions are paracentesis to relieve abdominal compartment syndrome and improve renal perfusion, cautious IV fluid resuscitation (avoiding aggressive hydration which can worsen third-spacing), strict fluid balance monitoring, and prophylactic LMWH for thromboembolism prevention. Cabergoline is preventative, not therapeutic, and laparoscopy is not indicated without signs of torsion.
key_points: OHSS is classified by severity: mild (abdominal bloating, ovarian size 5-12 cm), moderate (nausea, vomiting, ascites on ultrasound), severe (clinical ascites, oliguria, haemoconcentration Hct >0.45, creatinine >150, liver dysfunction). (Source: RANZCOG: Ovarian Hyperstimulation Syndrome)

Paracentesis is indicated for tense ascites causing respiratory compromise, oliguria, or significant pain. It provides rapid symptomatic relief and can improve renal function. (Source: KEMH: OHSS Guideline)
Thromboprophylaxis with prophylactic dose LMWH is mandatory for all inpatients with moderate-severe OHSS due to the hypercoagulable state. (Source: RANZCOG: Ovarian Hyperstimulation Syndrome)
IV fluids should be administered cautiously (strict input-output) as aggressive hydration can worsen third-space fluid accumulation. (Source: KEMH: OHSS Guideline)
explanation.pearls: In OHSS, the haematocrit is your most important guide: a rising Hct indicates worsening haemoconcentration and is a warning sign of severe disease, while a falling Hct with clinical improvement signals resolution. Oliguria despite adequate hydration = time for paracentesis.

stem: A 36-year-old G2P1 at 29+0 weeks gestation was diagnosed with gestational diabetes at 26 weeks on a 75 g OGTT (fasting 5.4 mmol/L, 1-hour 10.5 mmol/L, 2-hour 8.9 mmol/L). Her BMI is 32 kg/m². She has been managed with lifestyle modification (diet and exercise) for 3 weeks. Her fasting BGLs have consistently been 5.0-5.3 mmol/L (target <5.1 mmol/L) and her 2-hour postprandial BGLs are 6.2-6.6 mmol/L (target <6.7 mmol/L). An ultrasound at 28 weeks showed an estimated foetal weight on the 78th centile with a normal amniotic fluid index. She asks about medication options to improve her fasting glucose. She has no contraindications to metformin or insulin. She is concerned about the risk of needing insulin and the need for injections.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/76 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, fundal height 30 cm
data_table.Examination: Normal examination; no signs of macrosomia clinically
data_table.Investigations: OGTT: fasting 5.4, 1h 10.5, 2h 8.9 (all diagnostic); fasting BGLs 5.0-5.3 (target <5.1), 2h postprandial 6.2-6.6 (target <6.7); EFW 78th centile, AFI normal
data_table.Medications: Nil pharmacological; lifestyle measures for 3 weeks

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in managing her fasting hyperglycaemia?

options.text: Commence metformin 500 mg orally at night, titrate up to 1500 mg daily as tolerated, and continue diet and exercise measures
options.rationale: This woman has fasting BGLs consistently above the ADIPS target of <5.1 mmol/L despite 3 weeks of lifestyle modification. Metformin is an appropriate first-line pharmacological agent in Australian practice for GDM when lifestyle measures are insufficient. It is particularly effective for fasting hyperglycaemia by reducing hepatic gluconeogenesis. It is initiated at 500 mg nocte and titrated up. Insulin remains an option if metformin is not tolerated or fails to achieve targets. (Source: ADIPS: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus Guideline; RANZCOG: Gestational Diabetes)

options.text: Commence basal insulin (isophane or insulin detemir) at 0.1 units/kg at night, titrated according to fasting BGL, and cease metformin
