Management is supportive: CPR, ventilation, massive transfusion (red cells, platelets, FFP, cryoprecipitate/fibrinogen concentrate), and delivery. (Source: UKOSS; RANZCOG Guideline)
Risk factors: advanced maternal age, multiparity, meconium-stained liquor, operative delivery, prolonged labour. (Source: RANZCOG: Amniotic Fluid Embolism)
Survival requires rapid recognition and aggressive management; the maternal mortality rate remains 20-40%. (Source: UKOSS Study)
explanation.pearls: Amniotic fluid embolism is a diagnosis of exclusion. In any pregnant woman with sudden collapse, respiratory distress, and coagulopathy, treat for AFE while excluding other causes. Early and aggressive fibrinogen replacement is crucial.

stem: A 26-year-old G2P1 underwent suction curettage for a suspected molar pregnancy at 10 weeks gestation after an ultrasound showed a 'snowstorm' appearance and no foetal pole. Histopathology confirms a complete hydatidiform mole. She is keen to conceive again as soon as possible. Her hCG level 2 weeks post-evacuation is 2500 IU/L, down from 120,000 IU/L pre-operatively. She has no vaginal bleeding, and pelvic ultrasound shows an empty uterus with normal adnexa. What is the most appropriate advice regarding future pregnancy?

data_table.Vital signs: BP 115/70 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, Temp 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: No vaginal bleeding, uterus well involuted, no adnexal masses
data_table.Investigations: hCG 2500 IU/L (was 120,000); USS: empty uterus, normal adnexa; histology: complete mole

lead_in: What is the most appropriate advice regarding future pregnancy?

options.text: She may attempt pregnancy immediately once hCG becomes undetectable, as there is no increased risk in subsequent pregnancies
options.rationale: After a complete mole, hCG must be monitored monthly for at least 6 months after normalisation, and pregnancy should be avoided during that time because rising hCG from a new pregnancy could be confused with persistent trophoblastic disease. (Source: RANZCOG: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease)

options.text: She must avoid pregnancy for at least 6 months after achieving a normal hCG, and should use reliable contraception; monthly hCG monitoring is required during this period to detect persistent disease
options.rationale: Following evacuation of a complete mole, hCG is monitored fortnightly until negative, then monthly for 6-12 months (Australian protocol: 6 months after first normal level). Pregnancy should be avoided during this surveillance period to prevent confusion with rising hCG from a new pregnancy vs. persistent gestational trophoblastic disease. Effective contraception (COCP, IUD, implant) is recommended. (Source: RANZCOG: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease; KEMH: Molar Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: She should undergo a hysterectomy because complete moles have a 15-20% risk of progression to choriocarcinoma and future pregnancy is contraindicated
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is not required for a standard complete mole in a young woman who desires future fertility. The risk of persistent trophoblastic disease is managed with hCG surveillance; hysterectomy is reserved for older women who have completed childbearing or for treatment of invasive mole. (Source: RANZCOG: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease)

options.text: She requires a single dose of intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m² to prevent progression to persistent trophoblastic disease
options.rationale: Prophylactic chemotherapy is not routinely recommended for complete mole because most patients will spontaneously resolve with evacuation alone. It is reserved for patients at high risk of persistent disease (e.g., hCG >100,000 IU/L post-evacuation, age >40, or large for dates uterus). This patient does not yet meet those criteria. (Source: RANZCOG: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease)

options.text: She should undergo a CT chest and abdomen to stage the disease before planning pregnancy, as complete moles frequently metastasise to the lungs
options.rationale: Staging with CT is not routinely performed for uncomplicated complete mole with falling hCG. It is indicated if there is persistent or rising hCG, or clinical suspicion of metastatic disease (persistent vaginal bleeding, respiratory symptoms). (Source: RANZCOG: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease)

explanation.summary: Following evacuation of a complete hydatidiform mole, hCG must be monitored until normalised, then monthly for at least 6 months to detect persistent trophoblastic disease (which occurs in 15-20% of complete moles). Pregnancy should be avoided during the surveillance period because a rising hCG from a new pregnancy would be indistinguishable from malignant transformation. Reliable contraception is advised. Routine chemotherapy, hysterectomy, and staging CT are not required unless hCG plateaus, rises, or metastases are suspected.
key_points: Complete moles require post-evacuation hCG surveillance: fortnightly until negative, then monthly for 6 months. (Source: RANZCOG: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease)
Pregnancy should be avoided for at least 6 months after the first normal hCG; effective contraception is recommended. (Source: KEMH: Molar Pregnancy Guideline)
Persistent trophoblastic disease (rising or plateaued hCG) requires chemotherapy (methotrexate or actinomycin D). (Source: RANZCOG: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease)
Prophylactic chemotherapy is not routinely indicated for low-risk moles. (Source: RANZCOG: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease)
explanation.pearls: hCG monitoring after a molar pregnancy is the single most important element of follow-up. Any missed test may delay detection of choriocarcinoma, which is highly curable if caught early.

subtopic: Ovarian torsion in pregnancy – Imaging and surgical management
stem: A 30-year-old primigravida at 12+3 weeks gestation presents to the emergency department with a 6-hour history of acute onset severe right iliac fossa pain, associated with nausea and vomiting. The pain began suddenly while she was walking and has been constant since. She has no vaginal bleeding. On examination, she is in significant pain, HR 100 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg. The abdomen is tender in the right lower quadrant with voluntary guarding but no peritonism. A bedside transvaginal ultrasound shows a viable intrauterine pregnancy consistent with dates, a normal left ovary, and an enlarged right ovary measuring 7 cm with multiple peripheral follicles and absent internal blood flow on colour Doppler. The right ovarian vein is not well visualised. The appendix is not visualised. Her inflammatory markers are normal, and urine dipstick is negative. The on-call gynaecology registrar suspects ovarian torsion.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 100 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg, RR 18, Temp 36.5°C
data_table.Examination: Tender RIF, voluntary guarding, no peritonism, no cervical motion tenderness, uterus appropriate size
data_table.Investigations: TVUS: viable IUP, normal left ovary, right ovary 7 cm with peripheral follicles, absent internal Doppler flow; WCC 10.2, CRP 4

lead_in: What is the most appropriate definitive management?

options.text: Diagnostic laparoscopy with ovarian detorsion and ovarian cystectomy if a benign cyst is identified, with ovarian conservation
options.rationale: Ovarian torsion in pregnancy is a surgical emergency requiring urgent laparoscopy for detorsion to preserve ovarian function. Even if the ovary appears ischaemic, detorsion and conservation are recommended because ovarian recovery is common. Cystectomy can be performed for a benign-appearing cyst; oophorectomy is only considered if the ovary is frankly necrotic and non-viable. Laparoscopy is safe in the second trimester with appropriate port placement. (Source: RANZCOG: Adnexal Masses in Pregnancy; KEMH: Ovarian Torsion Guideline)

options.text: Oophorectomy via laparotomy, as the absent Doppler flow indicates irreversible ischaemia and the ovary cannot be salvaged
options.rationale: Absent Doppler flow does not reliably predict non-viability; many ovaries recover after detorsion. Ovarian conservation with detorsion is the standard of care, even with a black-bluish ovary. (Source: RANZCOG: Adnexal Masses in Pregnancy)

options.text: Conservative management with analgesia, antiemetics, and observation for 24 hours, as the pain may resolve spontaneously
options.rationale: Ovarian torsion will not resolve spontaneously; delay in surgical detorsion results in loss of ovarian function and potential infection. Urgent surgery is indicated. (Source: KEMH: Ovarian Torsion Guideline)

options.text: CT abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast to confirm the diagnosis before proceeding to surgery
options.rationale: CT is relatively contraindicated in pregnancy due to radiation exposure. The diagnosis of torsion is made clinically and with ultrasound. Further imaging delays surgery and may risk ovarian loss. (Source: RANZCOG: Adnexal Masses in Pregnancy)

options.text: Aspiration of the ovarian cyst under ultrasound guidance to reduce size and relieve the torsion without surgery
options.rationale: Aspiration does not detorse the pedicle and may cause cyst rupture, bleeding, or infection. Surgical detorsion is required. (Source: KEMH: Ovarian Torsion Guideline)

explanation.summary: Ovarian torsion in pregnancy is a gynaecological emergency requiring urgent laparoscopy with detorsion and ovarian conservation. The presence of a viable pregnancy does not contraindicate laparoscopic surgery in the first or second trimester. The ovary should be conserved regardless of its colour at surgery, as recovery is common. Doppler ultrasound showing absent flow is supportive but does not mandate oophorectomy. Delaying surgery risks ovarian necrosis and potential miscarriage.
key_points: Ovarian torsion in pregnancy: urgent laparoscopy with detorsion and ovarian conservation. (Source: RANZCOG: Adnexal Masses in Pregnancy)
Ovarian salvage is possible even with a dark, ischaemic-appearing ovary; oophorectomy is a last resort. (Source: KEMH: Ovarian Torsion Guideline)
Laparoscopy is safe in pregnancy up to 28 weeks with appropriate port placement (open entry, left upper quadrant if needed). (Source: RANZCOG: Adnexal Masses in Pregnancy)
Ultrasound features: enlarged ovary >5 cm, peripheral follicles, absent Doppler flow, and the 'whirlpool sign' of the twisted pedicle. (Source: KEMH: Ovarian Torsion Guideline)
explanation.pearls: In a pregnant patient with acute pelvic pain, always consider ovarian torsion, especially with an ovarian mass. The ovary is never too black to save – detort and observe.

subtopic: Third trimester bleeding – Placenta praevia vs vasa praevia
stem: A 29-year-old G2P1 at 36+0 weeks presents with a single episode of painless bright red vaginal bleeding. She has had no contractions. On speculum examination, the cervix is closed, and no active bleeding is seen. A bedside ultrasound shows an anterior placenta completely covering the internal os. The foetal heart rate is 145 bpm with normal variability. What is the most appropriate next step?

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender uterus, closed cervix on speculum, no active bleeding
data_table.Investigations: USS: major anterior placenta praevia; CTG reactive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Perform a digital vaginal examination to assess cervical dilation
options.rationale: Digital examination with known placenta praevia can precipitate catastrophic haemorrhage and is absolutely contraindicated. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Admit for observation, administer betamethasone, and plan delivery at 37 weeks if no further bleeding
options.rationale: A stable woman with major placenta praevia and a single self-limited bleed at 36 weeks should be admitted, and delivery planned at 37-38 weeks. Steroids are not indicated after 34+6 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

options.text: Induce labour with oxytocin immediately
options.rationale: Induction of labour in major placenta praevia would lead to massive haemorrhage as the placenta separates. Caesarean delivery is required. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

options.text: Discharge home with strict activity restriction and a follow-up ultrasound in 1 week
options.rationale: Any bleeding with major praevia warrants hospital admission, not discharge. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

options.text: Perform an amniotomy to exclude vasa praevia
options.rationale: Amniotomy would rupture membranes and potentially disrupt the placenta, causing catastrophic bleeding. It is contraindicated. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

explanation.summary: Major placenta praevia with painless bleeding requires admission and planning for caesarean delivery around 37-38 weeks if stable. Steroids are unnecessary at 36 weeks. Digital examination and amniotomy are contraindicated.
key_points: Never perform a digital vaginal examination when placenta praevia is known or suspected. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
Asymptomatic major praevia: elective caesarean at 37-38 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
Betamethasone is not indicated after 34+6 weeks gestation. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroid Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Speculum examination is safe; digital examination is not. The first rule of APH: never a digit until placenta praevia is excluded by ultrasound.

subtopic: Preterm birth – Antenatal corticosteroids at 23 weeks
stem: A 28-year-old primigravida at 23+4 weeks gestation presents with regular painful contractions and cervical dilation of 3 cm. She is otherwise stable. Should antenatal corticosteroids be administered?

data_table.Examination: Cervix 3 cm, bulging membranes
data_table.Investigations: USS: singleton, cephalic, AFI normal

lead_in: Should antenatal corticosteroids be given?

options.text: Yes, betamethasone 11.4 mg IM, two doses 24 hours apart
options.rationale: Antenatal corticosteroids are recommended between 23+0 and 34+6 weeks when delivery is anticipated within 7 days, including at periviable gestations from 23 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroid Guideline)

options.text: No, because the gestational age is below 24 weeks and benefit is uncertain
options.rationale: From 23+0 weeks, corticosteroids are recommended to reduce neonatal mortality and morbidity. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroid Guideline)

options.text: Yes, but only a single dose of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM
options.rationale: The full course of two doses is recommended to achieve maximal benefit. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroid Guideline)

options.text: No, steroids are only indicated after 24 weeks
options.rationale: Guidelines have extended the lower limit to 23+0 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroid Guideline)

options.text: Yes, dexamethasone 6 mg IM every 12 hours for 4 doses
options.rationale: Betamethasone is the preferred agent in Australia; dexamethasone is an alternative. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroid Guideline)

explanation.summary: Corticosteroids are recommended from 23 weeks gestation for women at risk of preterm delivery within 7 days, using betamethasone 11.4 mg IM, two doses 24 hours apart.
key_points: Antenatal corticosteroids indicated at 23+0 to 34+6 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroid Guideline)
Betamethasone 11.4 mg IM, 2 doses 24 hours apart. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroid Guideline)
explanation.pearls: At 23 weeks, every hour gained in utero and every dose of steroids matters.

stem: A 22-year-old nulliparous woman presents with irregular menstrual cycles (every 40-60 days) and mild facial acne. She is not hirsute. A pelvic ultrasound shows ovaries with 25 antral follicles per ovary. Serum testosterone is mildly elevated at 2.1 nmol/L. What is the most likely diagnosis?

data_table.Examination: No hirsutism, mild acne
data_table.Investigations: Testosterone 2.1 nmol/L (elevated), USS: polycystic ovaries (≥20 follicles per ovary), TSH normal, prolactin normal

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Polycystic ovary syndrome
options.rationale: She meets all three Rotterdam criteria: oligo-ovulation (irregular cycles), clinical and/or biochemical hyperandrogenism (elevated testosterone, acne), and polycystic ovaries on ultrasound. (Source: International Evidence-Based Guideline for PCOS 2018)

options.text: Congenital adrenal hyperplasia
options.rationale: CAH typically presents with more severe hyperandrogenism and elevated 17-hydroxyprogesterone. (Source: International PCOS Guideline 2018)

options.text: Hypothalamic amenorrhoea
options.rationale: Hypothalamic amenorrhoea is associated with low oestrogen and normal or low testosterone, not hyperandrogenism. (Source: International PCOS Guideline 2018)

options.text: Cushing syndrome
options.rationale: Cushing syndrome causes central obesity, striae, proximal myopathy, and hypertension, absent here. (Source: International PCOS Guideline 2018)

options.text: Idiopathic hirsutism
options.rationale: She has oligomenorrhoea and polycystic ovaries, not just hirsutism. (Source: International PCOS Guideline 2018)

explanation.summary: PCOS diagnosis requires two of three Rotterdam criteria: oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism, and polycystic ovaries, after excluding other causes. This woman meets all three.
key_points: PCOS diagnosis requires 2 of 3 criteria: oligo-ovulation, hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries on USS. (Source: International PCOS Guideline 2018)
Ultrasound threshold: ≥20 follicles per ovary using modern high-resolution probes. (Source: International PCOS Guideline 2018)
explanation.pearls: PCOS is a diagnosis of exclusion; always check TSH, prolactin, and 17-OH progesterone to rule out other endocrine disorders.

stem: A 35-year-old woman presents after her third consecutive miscarriage at 9 weeks. She had one previous live birth at term 4 years ago. She has no significant medical history. Which set of investigations is most appropriate?

data_table.Examination: Normal general and pelvic exam
data_table.Investigations: Products of conception: normal female karyotype

lead_in: What is the most appropriate set of investigations?

options.text: Anticardiolipin antibodies, lupus anticoagulant, beta-2 glycoprotein I antibodies, and a pelvic ultrasound for uterine anomalies
options.rationale: RANZCOG guidelines recommend testing for antiphospholipid syndrome (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, beta-2 glycoprotein I) and a pelvic ultrasound (3D if possible) to evaluate the uterine cavity. Parental karyotyping is not routinely recommended unless there is a family history or specific indication. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)

options.text: Antiphospholipid antibodies, parental karyotype, thrombophilia screen, and hysteroscopy
options.rationale: Parental karyotyping is not routinely recommended; thrombophilia screen (other than APS) is not indicated as it does not alter management. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)

options.text: Full blood count, thyroid function, and a glucose tolerance test
options.rationale: These are general health screens but do not address the specific causes of recurrent miscarriage. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)

options.text: Anti-Müllerian hormone, early follicular phase FSH, and oestradiol
options.rationale: These assess ovarian reserve, not the causes of recurrent pregnancy loss. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)

options.text: MTHFR gene mutation, factor V Leiden, and prothrombin gene mutation
options.rationale: Inherited thrombophilias are not routinely investigated for recurrent miscarriage in Australia as they do not alter management. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)

explanation.summary: The recommended investigation for recurrent miscarriage (≥3 consecutive losses) includes antiphospholipid antibody testing (to diagnose APS) and a pelvic ultrasound (to detect uterine anomalies). Parental karyotyping and inherited thrombophilia testing are not routinely recommended.
key_points: Routine investigations for recurrent miscarriage: antiphospholipid antibodies (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, beta-2 GPI) and pelvic ultrasound. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)
Parental karyotyping is only indicated if there is a family history of chromosomal rearrangements. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)
Inherited thrombophilias are not associated with recurrent miscarriage and should not be tested. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)
explanation.pearls: Antiphospholipid syndrome is the only treatable cause of recurrent miscarriage; low-dose aspirin and heparin can significantly improve live birth rates.

subtopic: Uterine rupture – Risk factors in VBAC
stem: A 32-year-old woman at 40+2 weeks is attempting vaginal birth after one prior lower-segment caesarean (VBAC). She is contracting 4 in 10 minutes, and CTG is reassuring. She requests epidural analgesia. Her labour is augmented with oxytocin at 6 mU/min for slow progress. She suddenly develops constant severe abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding. The CTG shows a prolonged foetal bradycardia. What is the most likely diagnosis?

data_table.Examination: Acute constant abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding, uterine tenderness, loss of contractions
data_table.Investigations: CTG: prolonged bradycardia, previous LSCS

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Uterine rupture
options.rationale: The combination of previous caesarean, oxytocin augmentation, constant severe pain, bleeding, loss of contractions, and foetal bradycardia is classic for uterine rupture. (Source: RANZCOG: VBAC Guideline)

options.text: Placental abruption
options.rationale: Abruption causes pain and bleeding but typically with hypertonic contractions, not loss of contractions; and the history of previous CS and oxytocin strongly points to rupture. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Cord prolapse
options.rationale: Cord prolapse presents with a palpable cord and variable decelerations, not constant pain and bleeding. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Normal labour pain with anxiety
options.rationale: The CTG bradycardia and bleeding exclude this. (Source: RANZCOG: VBAC Guideline)

options.text: Amniotic fluid embolism
options.rationale: AFE presents with acute hypoxia, collapse, and DIC, not isolated uterine pain and bleeding. (Source: RANZCOG: Amniotic Fluid Embolism)

explanation.summary: Uterine rupture is an obstetric emergency, especially in VBAC with oxytocin, presenting with constant abdominal pain, bleeding, loss of contractions, and foetal bradycardia. Immediate laparotomy is required.
key_points: Risk of uterine rupture in VBAC is higher with oxytocin augmentation (approximately 1.5-2%). (Source: RANZCOG: VBAC Guideline)
Classic signs: constant pain, vaginal bleeding, loss of contractions, foetal distress. (Source: RANZCOG: VBAC Guideline)
explanation.pearls: In VBAC, the epidural may mask pain from early uterine dehiscence; CTG monitoring is essential for early detection.

stem: A 34-year-old woman who had diet-controlled gestational diabetes diagnosed at 28 weeks delivered a healthy infant at 39 weeks. She is now 8 weeks postpartum. What is the most appropriate follow-up test?

data_table.Examination: Normal postpartum exam
data_table.Investigations: GDM on OGTT: fasting 5.4, 1h 10.6, 2h 9.0; no postpartum glucose test yet

lead_in: What is the most appropriate follow-up test?

options.text: 75 g oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) with fasting, 1h, and 2h glucose
options.rationale: ADIPS and RANZCOG recommend a 75 g OGTT at 6-12 weeks postpartum to screen for persistent diabetes or prediabetes after GDM. (Source: ADIPS: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus Guideline)

options.text: Fasting blood glucose only
options.rationale: Fasting alone misses impaired glucose tolerance, so an OGTT is required. (Source: ADIPS: GDM Guideline)

options.text: HbA1c
options.rationale: HbA1c is less sensitive for detecting diabetes in the early postpartum period and is not the recommended screening test. (Source: ADIPS: GDM Guideline)

options.text: No test required; she will be screened at her next routine health check at age 40
options.rationale: Women with GDM require postpartum screening due to high risk of type 2 diabetes. (Source: ADIPS: GDM Guideline)

options.text: Home glucose monitoring for 1 week
options.rationale: Formal OGTT is the standard, not home monitoring. (Source: ADIPS: GDM Guideline)

explanation.summary: All women with GDM should have a 75 g OGTT at 6-12 weeks postpartum to diagnose persistent diabetes or impaired glucose tolerance. Annual screening for type 2 diabetes is recommended thereafter.
key_points: Postpartum OGTT at 6-12 weeks post-delivery for all women with GDM. (Source: ADIPS: GDM Guideline)
HbA1c is not recommended for initial postpartum screening. (Source: ADIPS: GDM Guideline)
explanation.pearls: GDM is one of the strongest predictors of future type 2 diabetes; up to 50% of women develop diabetes within 5-10 years.

stem: A 16-year-old girl reports severe crampy lower abdominal pain with each period since menarche, missing school. Her cycles are regular. Examination is normal. What is the first-line pharmacological treatment?

data_table.Examination: Normal abdominal and external genital exam
data_table.Investigations: None

lead_in: What is the first-line pharmacological treatment?

options.text: Ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily, starting 1-2 days before menses
options.rationale: NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen) are first-line for primary dysmenorrhoea, reducing prostaglandin-mediated pain. They should be started before menses for best effect. The COCP is second-line if NSAIDs fail. (Source: RANZCOG: Dysmenorrhoea)

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill (COCP) in a 21/7 or continuous regimen
options.rationale: COCP is an effective second-line treatment if NSAIDs are not effective or tolerated. (Source: RANZCOG: Dysmenorrhoea)

options.text: Paracetamol 1 g four times daily
options.rationale: Paracetamol has weaker prostaglandin inhibition than NSAIDs and is less effective. (Source: RANZCOG: Dysmenorrhoea)

options.text: Laparoscopy to exclude endometriosis
options.rationale: In a teenager with classic primary dysmenorrhoea, empiric treatment is appropriate before invasive investigation. (Source: RANZCOG: Dysmenorrhoea)

options.text: Transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS)
options.rationale: TENS is a non-pharmacological adjunct, not first-line pharmacological therapy. (Source: RANZCOG: Dysmenorrhoea)

explanation.summary: Primary dysmenorrhoea is caused by prostaglandin release from the endometrium. NSAIDs like ibuprofen are first-line because they inhibit prostaglandin synthesis. Starting before menses improves efficacy. COCP is used if NSAIDs fail.
key_points: First-line: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400-600 mg TDS) started 1-2 days before menses. (Source: RANZCOG: Dysmenorrhoea)
COCP is an effective second-line therapy. (Source: RANZCOG: Dysmenorrhoea)
explanation.pearls: Timing matters with NSAIDs for dysmenorrhoea: start before the pain begins.

stem: A 28-year-old nulliparous woman has 2 years of severe dysmenorrhoea unresponsive to NSAIDs and the COCP, deep dyspareunia, and chronic pelvic pain. Pelvic ultrasound is normal. What is the gold standard for diagnosis?

data_table.Examination: Tender uterosacral ligaments, fixed retroverted uterus
data_table.Investigations: Pelvic USS: normal; no cysts
data_table.Medications: Trial of COCP and NSAIDs failed

lead_in: What is the gold standard for diagnosis?

options.text: Diagnostic laparoscopy with visualisation of endometriotic lesions
options.rationale: Laparoscopy with direct visualisation is the gold standard for diagnosing endometriosis, and histological biopsy confirms the diagnosis. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)

options.text: Transvaginal ultrasound
options.rationale: Ultrasound can detect ovarian endometriomas but cannot exclude superficial peritoneal endometriosis. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)

options.text: MRI pelvis
options.rationale: MRI is useful for deep infiltrating endometriosis but is not the first-line diagnostic tool; laparoscopy remains gold standard. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)

options.text: Serum CA-125 level
options.rationale: CA-125 is not diagnostic for endometriosis; it can be elevated in many conditions and has poor sensitivity and specificity. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)

options.text: Empiric treatment with a GnRH agonist for 3 months as a diagnostic test
options.rationale: Empiric GnRH agonists may provide symptom relief but do not provide a histological diagnosis, and they have significant side effects. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)

explanation.summary: Laparoscopy remains the gold standard for diagnosing endometriosis, allowing direct visualisation and biopsy. Transvaginal ultrasound and MRI can detect deep disease or endometriomas but cannot exclude superficial lesions.
key_points: Gold standard for endometriosis diagnosis: laparoscopy with histology. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)
Ultrasound and MRI are helpful but do not replace laparoscopy. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)
Empiric medical treatment without a diagnosis may delay definitive management. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)
explanation.pearls: Negative imaging does not rule out endometriosis; up to 50% of women with chronic pelvic pain and normal ultrasound have endometriosis found at laparoscopy.

subtopic: Symphysis pubis dysfunction – Management in pregnancy
stem: A 30-year-old primigravida at 32 weeks gestation reports severe pain over the pubic symphysis that radiates to the inner thighs, worse with walking, climbing stairs, and turning in bed. Examination reveals tenderness over the symphysis pubis and positive FABER test. What is the most appropriate first-line management?

data_table.Examination: Tender symphysis pubis, positive FABER, no neurological deficits
data_table.Investigations: USS: foetus well, cephalic

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Refer to a women's health physiotherapist for pelvic girdle exercises and a pelvic support belt, with simple analgesia (paracetamol)
options.rationale: Symphysis pubis dysfunction (pelvic girdle pain) is managed conservatively with physiotherapy focusing on pelvic stabilisation exercises, a pelvic support belt, and simple analgesia. Avoiding aggravating movements (abduction, asymmetric loading) is key. (Source: KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain in Pregnancy)

options.text: Induction of labour at 37 weeks to minimise further musculoskeletal damage
options.rationale: Delivery is not indicated for symphysis pubis dysfunction; it does not improve outcomes and may increase morbidity. (Source: KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain)

options.text: Prolonged bed rest for the remainder of the pregnancy
options.rationale: Bed rest is not recommended; it leads to deconditioning and does not improve pain. (Source: KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain)

options.text: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection into the symphysis pubis
options.rationale: Corticosteroid injections are reserved for severe refractory cases and are not first-line. (Source: KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain)

options.text: Prescribe opioid analgesia (e.g., oxycodone) for severe pain
options.rationale: Opioids are avoided in pregnancy due to foetal effects. Paracetamol and non-pharmacological measures are first-line. (Source: KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain)

explanation.summary: Pelvic girdle pain in pregnancy is managed with physiotherapy, a pelvic support belt, and simple analgesia. Avoidance of aggravating activities (stair climbing, asymmetric standing) is important. Bed rest and early delivery are not recommended.
key_points: First-line: physiotherapy, pelvic belt, paracetamol. (Source: KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain)
Avoid bed rest and opioids. (Source: KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain)
explanation.pearls: Symphysis pubis dysfunction does not typically affect mode of delivery; vaginal birth is usually possible with careful positioning.

stem: A 34-year-old primigravida at 28 weeks gestation with known uterine fibroids (largest 8 cm anterior fundal) presents with acute onset severe right upper quadrant and epigastric pain, vomiting, and localised tenderness. She is afebrile. Ultrasound shows a 9 cm fibroid with heterogeneous echotexture and no free fluid. The foetus is active with normal growth and Dopplers. What is the most likely diagnosis and appropriate management?

data_table.Examination: Severe tenderness over the uterine fundus, no peritonism, bowel sounds present
data_table.Investigations: USS: 9 cm fibroid with heterogeneous echotexture, no free fluid, foetus well

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis and appropriate management?

options.text: Red degeneration of fibroid; manage with analgesia (paracetamol, codeine if needed), rest, and monitor foetal wellbeing
options.rationale: Red (carneous) degeneration of a uterine fibroid occurs in the second and third trimesters due to outgrowth of blood supply, causing acute pain, vomiting, and tenderness. Management is conservative with analgesia, hydration, and foetal monitoring. Surgery is rarely needed. (Source: RANZCOG: Fibroids in Pregnancy)

options.text: Placental abruption; immediate delivery by caesarean section
options.rationale: Abruption causes uterine tenderness and pain but usually with vaginal bleeding and foetal distress, and USS would show retroplacental clot. The fibroid appearance is classic for degeneration. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Ovarian torsion; emergency laparoscopy
options.rationale: The pain is uterine/fundal and ultrasound shows a fibroid, not an ovarian mass. (Source: RANZCOG: Adnexal Masses in Pregnancy)

options.text: Acute cholecystitis; abdominal ultrasound with gallbladder assessment
options.rationale: RUQ pain could be cholecystitis, but the known fibroid and ultrasound showing heterogeneous fibroid point to red degeneration. (Source: RANZCOG: Fibroids in Pregnancy)

options.text: Preterm labour; tocolysis with nifedipine and betamethasone
options.rationale: There is no evidence of contractions or cervical change; the pain is localised, not labour. (Source: RANZCOG: Preterm Labour)

explanation.summary: Red degeneration of a fibroid presents with acute abdominal pain, vomiting, and tenderness over the fibroid. Conservative management with analgesia and monitoring is the standard, as it resolves spontaneously.
key_points: Red degeneration: acute pain, vomiting, tenderness over fibroid in second/third trimester. (Source: RANZCOG: Fibroids in Pregnancy)
Management is conservative: analgesia, rest, foetal monitoring. (Source: RANZCOG: Fibroids in Pregnancy)
Surgery is only indicated for complications like torsion of a pedunculated fibroid or infarction. (Source: RANZCOG: Fibroids in Pregnancy)
explanation.pearls: Red degeneration is common and benign – provide strong analgesia and reassure. Avoid unnecessary surgery.

stem: A 30-year-old G3P0A2 presents at 14 weeks gestation. She has had two previous pregnancies ending in painless cervical dilation and delivery at 20 and 22 weeks. A current transvaginal ultrasound shows cervical length 38 mm with a closed internal os. She has no risk factors for preterm birth other than her history. What is the most appropriate management to prevent recurrent preterm birth?

data_table.Examination: Normal pelvic exam, closed cervix
data_table.Investigations: TVS: cervical length 38 mm, closed os

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: History-indicated (prophylactic) cervical cerclage at 12-14 weeks
options.rationale: Women with a history of two or more second-trimester losses consistent with cervical insufficiency are offered a prophylactic (history-indicated) cerclage at 12-14 weeks, regardless of current cervical length. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical Insufficiency and Cerclage)

options.text: Serial transvaginal cervical length measurements every 2 weeks, with ultrasound-indicated cerclage if length <25 mm
options.rationale: This approach is for women with risk factors but no prior history of two mid-trimester losses. With two classic losses, prophylactic cerclage is indicated. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical Insufficiency)

options.text: Vaginal progesterone 400 mg daily until 36 weeks
options.rationale: Progesterone is used for short cervix or prior preterm birth, but cerclage is recommended for a history of cervical insufficiency. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical Insufficiency)

options.text: Bed rest and avoidance of strenuous activity
options.rationale: Bed rest has not been shown to prevent preterm birth and is not recommended. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical Insufficiency)

options.text: Emergency cerclage if cervical dilation occurs
options.rationale: Emergency cerclage is for women with exposed membranes, not for prophylactic prevention. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical Insufficiency)

explanation.summary: A history-indicated cerclage at 12-14 weeks is recommended for women with ≥2 second-trimester losses consistent with cervical insufficiency. This prevents recurrence and improves live birth rates.
key_points: History-indicated cerclage: ≥2 prior mid-trimester losses without another cause. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical Insufficiency)
Prophylactic cerclage is placed at 12-14 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical Insufficiency)
explanation.pearls: The best predictor of cervical insufficiency is a history of painless, progressive second-trimester cervical dilation – the current cervical length doesn't matter for the decision to place a history-indicated cerclage.

subtopic: Stillbirth – Risk factor identification and bereavement care
stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 at 36+2 weeks presents concerned about reduced foetal movements for the past 12 hours. She has no contractions, no vaginal bleeding, and no leakage of fluid. On examination, the uterus is soft, and foetal heart rate cannot be auscultated with Doppler. An urgent bedside ultrasound confirms foetal demise. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

data_table.Examination: No foetal heart auscultated, uterus size consistent with dates
data_table.Investigations: USS: no cardiac activity, foetal demise confirmed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Provide empathetic, clear communication to the parents about the loss, discuss options for delivery (induction vs expectant), and offer bereavement support and investigations to determine the cause
options.rationale: After confirming foetal demise, the immediate priority is breaking the news sensitively, discussing delivery options, and offering psychological support and investigations (maternal bloods, placental pathology, foetal autopsy). (Source: PSANZ/Safer Care Victoria: Stillbirth Care Bundle)

options.text: Arrange for immediate caesarean section to deliver the foetus
options.rationale: Caesarean section is not indicated for a stillbirth without a maternal indication; vaginal delivery is preferred. (Source: PSANZ: Stillbirth Guideline)

options.text: Discharge home with a plan to return for delivery at 40 weeks
options.rationale: Once foetal demise is confirmed, delivery should be offered within 24-48 hours after counselling. Prolonged retention increases the risk of DIC. (Source: PSANZ: Stillbirth Guideline)

options.text: Administer betamethasone to aid foetal lung maturation
options.rationale: No benefit of steroids with foetal demise. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroid Guideline)

options.text: Order a CTG to confirm the absence of foetal heart rate
options.rationale: Ultrasound has already confirmed foetal demise; a CTG is unnecessary. (Source: PSANZ: Stillbirth Guideline)

explanation.summary: When a stillbirth is diagnosed, the immediate priorities are sensitive communication, offering bereavement care, discussing delivery options (typically induction of labour), and offering investigations to determine cause.
key_points: After stillbirth diagnosis: empathetic communication, offer induction of labour within 24-48 hours, support. (Source: PSANZ/Safer Care Victoria)
Investigations: maternal bloods, placental pathology, foetal autopsy if consent. (Source: PSANZ/Safer Care Victoria)
explanation.pearls: The words used when telling a mother her baby has died will stay with her forever. Use clear language, not euphemisms, and allow silence.

stem: A 30-year-old multiparous woman at 38 weeks presents with sudden severe abdominal pain and board-like rigidity of the uterus. She has minimal vaginal bleeding. Foetal heart rate is 90 bpm with reduced variability. What is the most likely diagnosis?

data_table.Examination: Tense, tender, woody-hard uterus, minimal PV loss
data_table.Investigations: CTG: bradycardia 90 bpm, reduced variability

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Concealed placental abruption
options.rationale: The combination of severe pain, rigid uterus, minimal external bleeding, and foetal distress is classic for concealed abruption. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

options.text: Placenta praevia
options.rationale: Praevia typically presents with painless vaginal bleeding and a soft uterus. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

options.text: Uterine rupture
options.rationale: Uterine rupture often has a history of prior uterine surgery and loss of contractions. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

options.text: Labour dystocia
options.rationale: Labour pain is intermittent, not continuous with rigid uterus. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

options.text: Chorioamnionitis
options.rationale: Chorioamnionitis presents with fever, tender uterus, and purulent liquor, not acute severe pain and foetal distress without fever. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

explanation.summary: Concealed placental abruption presents with severe constant pain, a tense rigid uterus, and foetal distress with minimal external bleeding. Immediate delivery is indicated.
key_points: Concealed abruption: pain, rigid uterus, minimal vaginal bleeding, foetal distress. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
It is an obstetric emergency requiring immediate caesarean delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
explanation.pearls: In abruption, the amount of vaginal bleeding does not reflect the severity – the worst haemorrhage is concealed.

subtopic: Foetal monitoring – Deceleration types and significance
stem: A 28-year-old nullipara at 41 weeks is in labour with oxytocin augmentation. The CTG shows a baseline of 150 bpm with normal variability but repetitive late decelerations after each contraction. Contractions are 5 in 10 minutes. What is the most appropriate next step?

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal HR 100, BP 110/70
data_table.Examination: Cervix 6 cm, contractions 5:10
data_table.Investigations: CTG: baseline 150, normal variability, late decelerations

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Cease the oxytocin infusion, reposition the mother to left lateral, and consider acute tocolysis with terbutaline 250 mcg SC
options.rationale: Late decelerations indicate uteroplacental insufficiency. The first step is intrauterine resuscitation: cease oxytocin to reduce uterine activity, reposition, and consider tocolysis if contractions are hyperstimulated. (Source: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline)

options.text: Continue oxytocin at the same rate and prepare for immediate vaginal delivery
options.rationale: Continuing oxytocin in the presence of late decelerations may worsen foetal hypoxia. (Source: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline)

options.text: Perform an immediate caesarean section without further assessment
options.rationale: With normal variability and baseline, there is time for intrauterine resuscitation; immediate caesarean is not the first step. (Source: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline)

options.text: Apply a foetal scalp electrode and take a foetal scalp blood sample
options.rationale: Scalp blood sampling is used if the CTG remains pathological after resuscitation, not as the first step. (Source: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline)

options.text: Administer a maternal oxygen mask at 8 L/min and increase the oxytocin to achieve better contractions
options.rationale: Maternal oxygen has not been shown to improve foetal outcomes and increasing oxytocin would worsen the situation. (Source: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline)

explanation.summary: Late decelerations reflect transient foetal hypoxia due to uteroplacental insufficiency. Immediate steps are to reduce uterine activity (cease oxytocin), improve placental perfusion (left lateral), and consider tocolysis. Escalation to delivery depends on response.
key_points: Late decelerations: cease oxytocin, reposition, consider tocolysis. (Source: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline)
If CTG normalises, oxytocin may be cautiously restarted at lower dose. (Source: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Late decelerations with normal variability = time for conservative measures; late decelerations with reduced variability = immediate delivery.

stem: A 28-year-old primipara, 6 weeks postpartum, reports persistent low mood, anhedonia, poor sleep when the baby sleeps, and feelings of guilt. Her Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) score is 14. She is breastfeeding. She has no suicidal ideation. What is the most appropriate initial management?

data_table.Examination: Normal physical examination
data_table.Investigations: EPDS score 14, TSH normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Referral for psychological therapy (cognitive behavioural therapy or interpersonal therapy) and consider a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (e.g., sertraline 50 mg daily) after discussion of risks and benefits in breastfeeding
options.rationale: Moderate postpartum depression (EPDS 13-19) should be managed with psychological therapy and considered for antidepressant medication. Sertraline is the first-line SSRI in breastfeeding due to minimal excretion into breast milk. (Source: RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health; Beyond Blue Guideline)

options.text: Reassurance that 'baby blues' are normal and will resolve without treatment in a few weeks
options.rationale: Symptoms persisting beyond 2 weeks with functional impairment are not baby blues; they represent postpartum depression requiring treatment. (Source: RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health)

options.text: Immediate admission to a mother-baby psychiatric unit due to risk of harm to the baby
options.rationale: Inpatient admission is for severe depression, suicidal ideation, or psychosis, not moderate depression without safety concerns. (Source: RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health)

options.text: Start fluoxetine 20 mg daily and advise to stop breastfeeding
options.rationale: Fluoxetine has a long half-life and higher milk levels; sertraline is preferred in breastfeeding. Stopping breastfeeding is not necessary. (Source: RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health)

options.text: Advise increased exercise and socialisation only, and reassess in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Lifestyle measures are adjuncts but insufficient for moderate depression; active treatment with therapy and medication is recommended. (Source: RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health)

explanation.summary: Postpartum depression with an EPDS score of 14 is moderate. First-line treatment includes psychological therapy and consideration of an antidepressant. Sertraline is the preferred SSRI during breastfeeding. Untreated depression can affect maternal-infant bonding.
key_points: Postpartum depression: treat with CBT/interpersonal therapy ± SSRI (sertraline first-line in breastfeeding). (Source: RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health)
Baby blues resolve by day 10; symptoms persisting beyond 2 weeks are depression. (Source: RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health)
explanation.pearls: Sertraline is the SSRI of choice in breastfeeding due to minimal infant exposure. The mother's mental health is paramount – untreated depression also harms the infant.

subtopic: Major placenta praevia – Management after a self-limited bleed
stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 at 36+1 weeks gestation presents with a single episode of painless bright red vaginal bleeding, now settled. A transabdominal ultrasound confirms a major anterior placenta praevia completely covering the internal os. She is haemodynamically stable, the uterus is soft and non-tender, and the CTG is reactive with a normal baseline. There is no evidence of ongoing bleeding. What is the most appropriate plan?

data_table.Vital signs: HR 80 bpm, BP 115/70 mmHg, Temp 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Soft, non-tender uterus, no active bleeding on pad check
data_table.Investigations: Ultrasound: major anterior placenta praevia; CTG reactive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate plan?

options.text: Immediate delivery by category 1 caesarean section
options.rationale: Immediate delivery is indicated for ongoing haemorrhage, maternal or foetal instability. This woman is stable with a settled bleed. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

options.text: Admit to hospital for observation, administer betamethasone, and plan elective caesarean section at 37–38 weeks gestation if no further bleeding
options.rationale: For a stable major placenta praevia with a self-limited bleed at 36 weeks, admission for observation and planning for elective caesarean between 37 and 38 weeks is standard. Corticosteroids are not indicated beyond 34+6 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline; RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroid Guideline)

options.text: Induction of labour with artificial rupture of membranes and oxytocin
options.rationale: Vaginal delivery with a major placenta praevia would result in catastrophic haemorrhage. Caesarean delivery is mandatory. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

options.text: Discharge home on strict bed rest with instructions to return if bleeding recurs
options.rationale: Any bleeding from a major placenta praevia warrants inpatient observation; outpatient management is unsafe. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

options.text: Perform a digital vaginal examination to assess the cervix
options.rationale: Digital vaginal examination with a known or suspected placenta praevia can precipitate massive haemorrhage and is absolutely contraindicated. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

explanation.summary: A stable woman with a major placenta praevia and a self-limited bleed at 36 weeks should be admitted for observation. Elective caesarean delivery is planned for 37–38 weeks. Corticosteroids are not needed after 34+6 weeks. Digital examination is contraindicated.
key_points: Stable major placenta praevia: inpatient observation, delivery by caesarean at 37–38 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
Digital vaginal examination is contraindicated with placenta praevia. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Never a digit through a praevia – always confirm placental location before examining a woman with third-trimester bleeding.

stem: A 24-year-old woman at 8+2 weeks gestation presents with heavy vaginal bleeding and passage of tissue. On speculum examination, the os is open and products of conception are seen in the cervical canal. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an empty uterus with an endometrial thickness of 5 mm. She is haemodynamically stable. What is the most likely diagnosis?

data_table.Vital signs: HR 85 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg, Temp 36.8°C
data_table.Examination: Open os, tissue in canal; minimal bleeding now
data_table.Investigations: TVUS: empty uterus, endometrial thickness 5 mm

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Complete miscarriage
options.rationale: A complete miscarriage has a closed os and an empty uterus; this woman has an open os with tissue visible. (Source: RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Loss)

options.text: Incomplete miscarriage
options.rationale: An open os with products of conception visible or retained in the uterus characterises an incomplete miscarriage. (Source: RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Loss)

options.text: Threatened miscarriage
options.rationale: In a threatened miscarriage, the os is closed and the pregnancy is viable. (Source: RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Loss)

options.text: Ectopic pregnancy
options.rationale: An ectopic pregnancy would typically show an empty uterus with an adnexal mass, not tissue in the cervical canal. (Source: RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Loss)

options.text: Molar pregnancy
options.rationale: A molar pregnancy would show a 'snowstorm' appearance on ultrasound, not an empty uterus with a thin endometrial stripe. (Source: RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Loss)

explanation.summary: Incomplete miscarriage is diagnosed when there is an open cervical os with retained products of conception. Complete miscarriage shows a closed os and an empty uterus. Threatened miscarriage has a closed os and a viable pregnancy.
key_points: Incomplete miscarriage: open os, products of conception visible or retained. (Source: RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Loss)
Management options include expectant, medical, or surgical evacuation depending on clinical stability. (Source: RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Loss)
explanation.pearls: The appearance of the cervical os on speculum examination is the key to differentiating complete from incomplete miscarriage.

subtopic: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes – Chorioamnionitis risk
stem: A 24-year-old G2P1 at 29+4 weeks gestation presents with a sudden gush of clear fluid and ongoing trickling. She is afebrile and has no contractions. Speculum examination confirms pooling of clear fluid with positive nitrazine test. Cervix is closed and long. CTG is reassuring. She received a course of betamethasone at 27 weeks for a threatened preterm labour that resolved. What is the most appropriate antibiotic regimen for prolonging latency and reducing neonatal morbidity?

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.8°C, HR 85 bpm, BP 110/65 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Pooling of fluid, closed cervix, no uterine tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Positive nitrazine, CTG reactive, WCC 11.2, CRP 8

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral erythromycin 250 mg four times daily for 10 days
options.rationale: The ORACLE I trial demonstrated that erythromycin prolongs pregnancy and reduces neonatal morbidity in PPROM. Co-amoxiclav is avoided due to increased risk of neonatal necrotising enterocolitis. A single rescue course of betamethasone should also be given as the prior course was >7 days ago. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline; ORACLE I Trial)

options.text: IV ampicillin 2 g 6-hourly plus IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily for 48 hours, then oral amoxicillin for 5 days
options.rationale: This regimen is used for chorioamnionitis or established infection, not for latency in uncomplicated PPROM. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily for 10 days
options.rationale: Co-amoxiclav is associated with an increased risk of neonatal necrotising enterocolitis and should be avoided in PPROM. (Source: ORACLE I Trial)

options.text: No antibiotics are required as she is afebrile
options.rationale: Antibiotics are recommended in PPROM to prolong latency and reduce chorioamnionitis and neonatal morbidity. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline)

options.text: Intramuscular ceftriaxone 1 g daily for 7 days
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone alone is not the standard regimen for PPROM latency; erythromycin is recommended. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline)

explanation.summary: In PPROM, oral erythromycin for 10 days is recommended to prolong latency and reduce neonatal morbidity (ORACLE I trial). Co-amoxiclav is avoided. A rescue course of betamethasone is indicated if >7 days since the last course.
key_points: Antibiotic of choice for PPROM: erythromycin 250 mg QID for 10 days. (Source: ORACLE I Trial; RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline)
Avoid co-amoxiclav due to NEC risk. (Source: ORACLE I Trial)
explanation.pearls: In PPROM, erythromycin is the magic bullet – cheap, effective, and safe.

subtopic: HELLP syndrome – Diagnosis and urgent delivery timing
stem: A 34-year-old G2P1 at 30+2 weeks gestation with known chronic hypertension presents with worsening epigastric pain, nausea, and headache. Her blood pressure is 160/105 mmHg despite oral labetalol 200 mg three times daily. Urinalysis shows 3+ protein. Laboratory results reveal: haemoglobin 105 g/L, platelets 55 ×10⁹/L, aspartate aminotransferase (AST) 280 U/L, alanine aminotransferase (ALT) 240 U/L, lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) 650 U/L, haptoglobin <0.3 g/L, serum creatinine 115 µmol/L, urate 0.52 mmol/L. Peripheral blood film shows schistocytes. CTG shows a baseline of 150 bpm with reduced variability and occasional late decelerations. She has received the first dose of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM 6 hours ago. Intravenous hydralazine has been given for blood pressure control. The obstetric and anaesthetic teams are at the bedside. What is the most appropriate plan regarding delivery?

data_table.Vital signs: BP 160/105, HR 98 bpm, RR 20, SpO₂ 96%, Temp 36.8°C
data_table.Examination: Epigastric tenderness, hyperreflexia, 3 beats clonus, uterine fundus soft
data_table.Investigations: Plt 55, AST 280, ALT 240, LDH 650, haptoglobin <0.3, Cr 115; CTG: reduced variability, late decelerations; Betamethasone dose 1 given 6h ago
data_table.Medications: Labetalol 200 mg TDS PO, IV hydralazine

lead_in: What is the most appropriate plan regarding delivery?

options.text: Administer the second dose of betamethasone at 24 hours and aim for delivery at 48 hours from the first dose, provided maternal and foetal conditions remain stable
options.rationale: This woman has HELLP syndrome with severe features: platelets <100 ×10⁹/L, AST >70 U/L, LDH >600 U/L, and evidence of haemolysis (low haptoglobin, schistocytes). Delivery is indicated within 24 hours, not delayed for completion of the steroid course. (Source: SOMANZ Guidelines: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy)

options.text: Proceed to delivery within 24 hours, preferably after administering the second dose of betamethasone at 12 hours from the first if maternal and foetal status permit, but do not delay beyond 24 hours
options.rationale: HELLP syndrome is a progressive condition that can deteriorate rapidly. Delivery is the definitive treatment. While completing the steroid course is ideal, in the presence of worsening features (platelets dropping, rising transaminases, renal impairment, foetal compromise on CTG), delivery should not be postponed beyond 24 hours. A second dose of betamethasone can be given at 12–24 hours if feasible, but not at the expense of maternal safety. (Source: SOMANZ Guidelines; RANZCOG Pre-eclampsia Guideline)

options.text: Give the second dose of betamethasone now (6 hours after first) and deliver immediately after the infusion, as expedited delivery is required regardless of steroid status
options.rationale: The recommended interval between betamethasone doses is 24 hours, though a minimal interval of 12 hours is sometimes considered. Giving the second dose at 6 hours does not optimise neonatal benefit. Delivery should be expedited but does not need to be immediate if the mother is stabilised and foetal monitoring is continuous; aiming for 12–24 hours is acceptable. (Source: SOMANZ Guideline)

options.text: Commence magnesium sulphate infusion for foetal neuroprotection and expectantly manage for 48 hours to complete the steroid course, as long as platelets remain above 50 ×10⁹/L
options.rationale: Expectant management beyond 24 hours in HELLP syndrome with foetal compromise is not recommended due to high risk of eclampsia, hepatic rupture, and abruptio placentae. (Source: SOMANZ Guideline)

options.text: Perform immediate caesarean delivery under general anaesthesia without waiting for the second betamethasone dose
options.rationale: Immediate delivery is indicated if there is acute deterioration, eclampsia, or non-reassuring foetal status that does not respond to intrauterine resuscitation. With current CTG abnormalities, close monitoring and expedited delivery within 12–24 hours is reasonable, but not emergent at this moment. (Source: SOMANZ Guideline)

explanation.summary: HELLP syndrome is a life-threatening variant of pre-eclampsia requiring delivery. While completion of the corticosteroid course is desirable for neonatal benefit, maternal safety is paramount. In the presence of laboratory deterioration and foetal CTG abnormalities, delivery should be accomplished within 24 hours, ideally after the second steroid dose if feasible at 12 hours. Magnesium sulphate is indicated for seizure prophylaxis, not solely for neuroprotection.
key_points: HELLP syndrome: deliver within 24 hours; delaying for steroids should not compromise maternal wellbeing. (Source: SOMANZ Guidelines)
Severe features: platelets <100, AST ≥70, LDH >600, haemolysis, renal dysfunction. (Source: RANZCOG Pre-eclampsia Guideline)
Foetal CTG abnormalities necessitate close monitoring and potential urgent delivery. (Source: RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline)
explanation.pearls: HELLP can deteriorate in hours; repeated laboratory surveillance is critical to timing delivery.

stem: A 26-year-old primigravida at 38 weeks gestation with severe pre-eclampsia is being managed with intravenous magnesium sulphate (4 g loading dose, then 1 g/hour) and oral labetalol 200 mg BD. Four hours after commencing the magnesium infusion, she has a generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting 3 minutes. The seizure self-terminates. On examination, she is postictal with a GCS of 10. Blood pressure is 150/100 mmHg, respiratory rate 16, patellar reflexes present but reduced. The magnesium level 1 hour ago was 1.8 mmol/L (therapeutic range 1.7–3.5 mmol/L). Urine output has been 30 mL/hr. The foetal heart rate is 130 bpm with normal variability. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

data_table.Vital signs: BP 150/100, HR 90 bpm, RR 16, SpO₂ 95%, GCS 10, patellar reflexes reduced
data_table.Examination: Postictal, no focal neurological signs, fundus soft
data_table.Investigations: Mg²⁺ 1.8 mmol/L, urine output 30 mL/hr, CTG reactive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Administer an additional 2 g intravenous bolus of magnesium sulphate over 5–10 minutes and continue the maintenance infusion at 1 g/hour
options.rationale: A recurrent eclamptic seizure while on magnesium therapy (therapeutic level) indicates a need for an additional bolus of 2 g over 5–10 minutes, with close monitoring for magnesium toxicity. A further bolus of 2 g may be repeated if seizures persist. Intubation and phenytoin are reserved for refractory cases. (Source: SOMANZ Guidelines: Eclampsia; KEMH Eclampsia Guideline)

options.text: Stop the magnesium sulphate infusion immediately, administer intravenous calcium gluconate 1 g for magnesium toxicity, and commence intravenous phenytoin 15 mg/kg loading dose
options.rationale: The magnesium level is within the therapeutic range, not toxic. Calcium gluconate is indicated for magnesium toxicity (absent reflexes, respiratory depression). Phenytoin is less effective than magnesium for eclampsia. (Source: SOMANZ Guideline)

options.text: Give intravenous midazolam 5 mg to terminate the seizure and arrange an urgent non-contrast CT head to exclude intracranial haemorrhage
options.rationale: While CT head may be indicated if there are focal neurological signs or atypical presentation, the immediate management of recurrent eclamptic seizure is an additional magnesium bolus, not benzodiazepine. (Source: SOMANZ Guideline)

options.text: Prepare for immediate caesarean delivery, as recurrent seizures despite magnesium indicate impending foetal demise
options.rationale: Delivery is the definitive treatment for pre-eclampsia/eclampsia, but the immediate priority is maternal stabilisation and seizure control. Delivery can be expedited once she is stable. (Source: SOMANZ Guideline)

options.text: Administer intravenous labetalol 20 mg to urgently lower blood pressure to below 140/90 mmHg, as hypertension is the trigger for seizure recurrence
options.rationale: While blood pressure control is important, the immediate priority is to stop the seizure with additional magnesium, not antihypertensives. (Source: SOMANZ Guideline)

explanation.summary: Recurrent eclamptic seizure on therapeutic magnesium warrants an additional 2 g IV bolus, repeated once if needed. Calcium gluconate is for magnesium toxicity, not for therapeutic levels. Delivery is planned after stabilisation. Blood pressure control is secondary.
key_points: For recurrent eclampsia: additional MgSO₄ 2 g IV bolus, then reassess. (Source: SOMANZ Guideline)
Magnesium toxicity: absent patellar reflexes, respiratory depression, hypotension. (Source: KEMH Eclampsia Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Magnesium therapy for eclampsia is titrated to clinical response, not serum levels. Recurrent seizures = need for more magnesium, not less.

subtopic: Foetal growth restriction – Timing of delivery with AEDF
stem: A 31-year-old primigravida at 30+6 weeks with early-onset foetal growth restriction (estimated foetal weight below the 3rd centile) has been managed as an inpatient for the past week. Today's umbilical artery Doppler shows absent end-diastolic flow (AEDF), middle cerebral artery Doppler shows a low pulsatility index (brain-sparing), and ductus venosus Doppler shows a positive a-wave. Amniotic fluid index is 6.5 cm. CTG demonstrates a baseline of 145 bpm, reduced variability (4 bpm), and occasional spontaneous accelerations, but no decelerations. She received a full course of betamethasone (two doses 24 hours apart) 8 days ago. What is the most appropriate timing for delivery?

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/76 mmHg, HR 78 bpm
data_table.Examination: No proteinuria, fundal height 26 cm
data_table.Investigations: UA Doppler: AEDF; MCA: low PI; DV: positive a-wave; AFI 6.5 cm; CTG: reduced variability, no decelerations; Betamethasone completed 8 days ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate timing for delivery?

options.text: Administer a rescue course of betamethasone and deliver within 24 hours, given AEDF and reduced CTG variability
options.rationale: A rescue course is indicated if delivery is anticipated within 7 days and the prior course was >7 days ago. However, with a positive DV a-wave and no CTG decelerations, delivery can be planned within 24–48 hours after the rescue course, not immediately. (Source: RANZCOG: Foetal Growth Restriction Guideline)

options.text: Deliver within 24–48 hours, giving a rescue course of betamethasone as the prior course was 8 days ago, and commence magnesium sulphate for foetal neuroprotection
options.rationale: AEDF with reduced CTG variability but positive DV a-wave and no decelerations indicates delivery within 24–48 hours. A rescue steroid course is indicated because >7 days have passed and delivery is anticipated. Magnesium sulphate is given for neuroprotection (<32 weeks). (Source: RANZCOG: Foetal Growth Restriction Guideline; KEMH FGR Guideline)

options.text: Immediate caesarean delivery within 2 hours, as AEDF with reduced variability indicates acute foetal decompensation
options.rationale: Immediate delivery is for reversed EDF, abnormal DV (absent/reversed a-wave), or recurrent CTG decelerations. This foetus has a positive DV a-wave and no decelerations, allowing 24–48 hours. (Source: RANZCOG: FGR Guideline)

options.text: Continue expectant management with daily CTG and repeat Dopplers in 72 hours, as a positive DV a-wave is reassuring
options.rationale: AEDF with reduced CTG variability is an indication for delivery, not continued observation. (Source: RANZCOG: FGR Guideline)

options.text: Deliver at 34 weeks once the rescue steroid course is effective, as AEDF alone is not an indication for preterm delivery
options.rationale: AEDF at 30 weeks is an indication for delivery within 24–48 hours; waiting until 34 weeks would risk foetal death. (Source: RANZCOG: FGR Guideline)

explanation.summary: In FGR with AEDF, brain-sparing, reduced CTG variability but positive DV a-wave and no decelerations, delivery is indicated within 24–48 hours. A rescue course of betamethasone is given if >7 days since the last course. Magnesium sulphate is indicated for neuroprotection at <32 weeks.
key_points: AEDF + reduced CTG variability + positive DV a-wave = deliver in 24–48 h. (Source: RANZCOG: FGR Guideline)
Rescue steroids if >7 days since last course and delivery expected within 7 days. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroid Guideline)
Magnesium sulphate for neuroprotection if <32 weeks and delivery imminent. (Source: KEMH Guideline)
explanation.pearls: The ductus venosus a-wave is the last Doppler to become abnormal; a positive a-wave allows a brief window to give steroids, but close monitoring is mandatory.

subtopic: Amniotic fluid embolism – Coagulopathy management after arrest
stem: A 34-year-old G2P1 at 39+2 weeks gestation is in spontaneous labour with a prolonged second stage and meconium-stained liquor. The membranes ruptured 5 hours ago. She suddenly becomes agitated, complains of difficulty breathing, and then collapses with loss of consciousness. The cardiac monitor shows pulseless electrical activity. Cardiopulmonary resuscitation is commenced immediately, and an emergency caesarean section is performed in the labour ward 6 minutes after the arrest. A live infant is delivered with Apgars of 3, 5, and 7. Following delivery, the mother has return of spontaneous circulation but is noted to have diffuse oozing from the surgical wound, venipuncture sites, and the uterus is atonic with heavy bleeding. Laboratory investigations drawn 20 minutes after arrest show: haemoglobin 62 g/L, platelets 18 ×10⁹/L, fibrinogen 0.4 g/L, activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT) >120 seconds, prothrombin time (PT) >60 seconds, D-dimer >100,000 ng/mL. A massive transfusion protocol has been activated and the patient is receiving packed red blood cells and fresh frozen plasma. She has received 4 units of FFP and 6 units of RBCs, but continues to bleed profusely. Her repeat fibrinogen level is 0.6 g/L. What is the most critical blood product to administer next to improve haemostasis?

data_table.Vital signs: HR 130 bpm, BP 80/45 mmHg on vasopressors, SpO₂ 94% on ventilator
data_table.Examination: Diffuse oozing surgical wound and venipuncture sites, atonic uterus, ongoing massive haemorrhage
data_table.Investigations: Hb 62, Plt 18, fibrinogen 0.4 (repeat 0.6), APTT >120, PT >60, D-dimer >100,000
data_table.Medications: MTP activated, 6 units RBCs, 4 units FFP given

lead_in: What is the most critical blood product to administer next?

options.text: Cryoprecipitate 10 units (or fibrinogen concentrate 4 g) to raise the fibrinogen level above 2.0 g/L
options.rationale: Amniotic fluid embolism causes a consumptive coagulopathy with severe hypofibrinogenaemia. Fibrinogen is the first clotting factor to reach critically low levels. Guidelines recommend maintaining fibrinogen >2.0 g/L during obstetric haemorrhage; cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate is the most efficient way to achieve this. (Source: RANZCOG: Amniotic Fluid Embolism; SOMANZ/ANZCA Guidelines for Massive Transfusion)

options.text: Pooled platelets 1 adult dose to correct the severe thrombocytopenia
options.rationale: Platelet transfusion is indicated when the platelet count is <50 ×10⁹/L in the context of active bleeding, but the immediate life-threatening defect is the extremely low fibrinogen, which must be corrected first to stabilise clot formation. (Source: ANZCA Massive Transfusion Guidelines)

options.text: More fresh frozen plasma 15 mL/kg to replace all clotting factors simultaneously
options.rationale: FFP contains fibrinogen but at low concentration; large volumes would be needed to raise fibrinogen adequately, risking volume overload. Cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate provides a concentrated source. (Source: RANZCOG: Amniotic Fluid Embolism)

options.text: Prothrombinex-HT 25 IU/kg to reverse the elevated INR and APTT
options.rationale: Prothrombinex concentrates factors II, IX, and X but contains negligible fibrinogen. It is not the priority when fibrinogen is <1.0 g/L. (Source: ANZCA Massive Transfusion Guidelines)

options.text: Intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g bolus followed by infusion, as hyperfibrinolysis is the dominant pathology
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid is an adjunct in PPH but does not replace the depleted fibrinogen. Severe hypofibrinogenaemia requires replacement, not just antifibrinolytics. (Source: WOMAN Trial; RANZCOG: Amniotic Fluid Embolism)

explanation.summary: Amniotic fluid embolism is characterised by cardiovascular collapse, respiratory failure, and consumptive coagulopathy. Severe hypofibrinogenaemia (<1.0 g/L) is a hallmark and requires aggressive replacement with cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate. FFP alone is insufficient. Tranexamic acid and prothrombinex are adjuncts, not substitutes for fibrinogen. Platelets are needed when counts are very low but fibrinogen correction takes priority.
key_points: AFE causes profound hypofibrinogenaemia; fibrinogen replacement is critical. (Source: RANZCOG: Amniotic Fluid Embolism)
Maintain fibrinogen >2.0 g/L in obstetric haemorrhage. (Source: ANZCA Massive Transfusion Guidelines)
Cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate provides concentrated fibrinogen. (Source: ANZCA Guidelines)
explanation.pearls: In AFE, the fibrinogen level is the best predictor of severe haemorrhage and the most urgent coagulation factor to replace.

stem: A 36-year-old G5P3 with two previous lower-segment caesarean sections and one first-trimester curettage has a known anterior major placenta praevia. Antenatal ultrasound and MRI suggest placenta accreta without frank percreta. She has expressed a strong desire for future fertility. At 36+0 weeks, she experiences a self-limited bleed and an elective caesarean is performed at 37+0 weeks. The surgical team includes a gynaecological oncologist. At delivery, a high transverse uterine incision is made above the placental edge. After delivery of the infant, the placenta does not separate spontaneously. Gentle controlled cord traction is unsuccessful, and the myometrium over the lower segment is noted to be thin and vascular with the placenta invading the uterine wall. Attempts at manual removal cause torrential haemorrhage of approximately 1.5 L in 3 minutes. The uterus is otherwise well-contracted, and the upper segment is normal. The patient remains haemodynamically stable with aggressive volume replacement and blood products. She reiterates her desire for uterine conservation if possible. What is the most appropriate surgical approach?

data_table.Vital signs: HR 120 bpm, BP 95/60 mmHg, SpO₂ 98%
data_table.Examination: Uterine lower segment invasion, torrential haemorrhage on attempted removal, normal upper segment
data_table.Investigations: Antenatal imaging: accreta without percreta; estimated blood loss 1.5 L in 3 min

lead_in: What is the most appropriate surgical approach?

options.text: Leave the placenta in situ, close the uterus, and consider postoperative methotrexate or uterine artery embolisation, with delayed hysterectomy if needed
options.rationale: For a woman with placenta accreta who desires future fertility, leaving the placenta in situ is a recognised conservative option. Postoperative methotrexate may accelerate placental involution. Hysterectomy is the definitive treatment but sacrifices fertility. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum; FIGO Consensus Guidelines on PAS)

options.text: Proceed immediately to total abdominal hysterectomy with the placenta left in situ, as this is the safest management for accreta
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is the standard management but does not preserve fertility. In a patient requesting conservation, leaving the placenta in situ is a valid alternative if the bleeding is controlled. (Source: RANZCOG: PAS Guideline)

options.text: Perform a uterine compression suture (B-Lynch) and uterine artery ligation to control bleeding, then close
options.rationale: Compression sutures and vessel ligation are useful for atonic PPH, but with placenta accreta, the bleeding is from the placental bed invasion, and these measures are unlikely to control it without removing the placenta. (Source: RANZCOG: PAS Guideline)

options.text: Attempt to remove the placenta piecemeal with sharp dissection and oversew the bleeding sites
options.rationale: Piecemeal removal risks incomplete evacuation, ongoing haemorrhage, and uterine perforation. Leaving the placenta in situ is safer for conservation. (Source: FIGO PAS Guidelines)

options.text: Perform a caesarean hysterectomy but preserve the ovaries and refer for surrogacy
options.rationale: This does not preserve fertility; the patient desires uterine conservation. (Source: RANZCOG: PAS Guideline)

explanation.summary: For a haemodynamically stable patient with placenta accreta who desires future fertility, leaving the placenta in situ with postoperative methotrexate and close monitoring is a recognised conservative strategy. Hysterectomy is definitive but eliminates fertility. Piecemeal removal and compression sutures are not appropriate.
key_points: Conservative management of PAS: leave placenta in situ, consider methotrexate, monitor for infection and bleeding. (Source: FIGO PAS Guidelines)
Hysterectomy remains the gold standard for uncontrolled haemorrhage or completed families. (Source: RANZCOG: PAS Guideline)
explanation.pearls: The decision between hysterectomy and conservation in PAS must be individualised; preoperative counselling about fertility desires is essential.

subtopic: Foetal growth restriction – Reversed end-diastolic flow
stem: A 29-year-old primigravida with early-onset foetal growth restriction (estimated foetal weight <3rd centile) is being monitored as an inpatient at 29+3 weeks. Serial ultrasound today shows new reversed end-diastolic flow (REDF) in the umbilical artery. Previous Dopplers 3 days ago showed absent end-diastolic flow. Ductus venosus Doppler shows a positive a-wave. Amniotic fluid index is 5.2 cm (oligohydramnios). CTG shows a baseline of 140 bpm, reduced variability (3–5 bpm), and no accelerations, but no decelerations. She received a full course of betamethasone 10 days ago. She is on oral labetalol for chronic hypertension. On examination, blood pressure is 135/85 mmHg, no proteinuria. The neonatal team has been informed and is ready. What is the most appropriate timing of delivery?

data_table.Vital signs: BP 135/85, HR 76 bpm
data_table.Examination: Fundal height 25 cm, soft uterus
data_table.Investigations: UA: REDF; DV: positive a-wave; AFI 5.2 cm; CTG: reduced variability, no accelerations, no decelerations; Steroids completed 10 days ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate timing of delivery?

options.text: Deliver within 24 hours, administering a rescue course of betamethasone as the prior course was over 7 days ago, and commence magnesium sulphate for foetal neuroprotection
